item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Listen Labs 不是"更便宜的市场调研"，而是"更贵的市场调研"
表面叙事很诱人——AI 替代人工、规模化降本、Bain/McKinsey 颤抖。但 Alfred 自己泄露了真相："我们能向客户收取数十万美元，去跟八个国家的 20 位医生交谈。"这不是消费级 SaaS 的下沉故事，而是**让"过去做不到的事"变得可做**——而非让"做得到的事"变得便宜。
这里有个让人意外的对照：**Listen Labs 的真正竞争对手不是 Qualtrics，而是"不存在"**。是那些公司本来就不会做的研究、那些根本不会被问出来的问题、那些被默认为"不值得调查"的 99% 决策。当你把单位访谈成本降到接近零，需求曲线下方那片巨大的灰色三角形（消费者剩余）就被照亮了——而这片灰色，过去甚至没有名字。
## 二、隐藏的护城河不在模型，而在 Eval：一个跨领域的暗线
Alfred 说漏了一句最关键的话："我们的 eval 从 20% 爬到了 85%。然后我们设计了一个**更难的 eval**，现在又回到 20%。"
**垂直 AI 公司的真正护城河不是数据、不

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] NVIDIA黃仁勳演講懶人包！7大戰略未來十年投資機會一次看飽！【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：黄仁勋悄悄宣布了"CPU 的物种灭绝"

表面上，整场演讲被解读为"Vera Rubin 上市 + 七大战略"。但真正的核弹被埋在一句话里："过去所有的 CPU 都是为人类设计的，Vera 是第一颗为 agent 设计的 CPU。"

这意味着什么？过去 50 年，整个云计算的商业模式建立在一个假设之上——人类用秒计时，按 vCPU core/小时租用。AWS、Azure、阿里云的整个定价骨架都依赖"人感知不到的延迟 = 可压缩成本"。

但 agent 活在纳秒世界里。当一个 agent 等待 100ms，它就等于损失了一次工具调用、一次推理跳。Jensen 真正说的是：云计算的计费单位即将从"core/hour"被迫切换到"token/watt"——这是自 2006 年 EC2 诞生以来云行业最大的一次单位制崩塌。

讽刺的是，Intel 和 AMD 在这场演讲中一次都没被提到，但他们才是真正被掀桌的人。

二、跨领域连接：他正在把"计算机产业"重写成"工业革命的第三幕"

把演讲中三个看似无关的细节连起来： - "1 gigawatt means 1 gigawatt"（电力是硬约束） - "compute is revenue, throughput per watt is revenue"（瓦特是营收单位） - 生态从"软件开发者"扩张到"发电机、冷却系统、电网供应商"

这是一个经济学坐标系的迁移。请注意一个反直觉的等价：

Nvidia 现在卖的不是芯片，是"每瓦特的边际生产力"——这正是 1769 年瓦特改良蒸汽机时发明的概念单位。

更深的隐喻：Jensen 把数据中心讲成"AI 工厂"不是修辞，而是会计准则的迁移。AI 工厂的折旧表、ROIC、产能利用率，正在向 19 世纪的纺织厂、20 世纪的炼油厂靠拢。这意味着分析 Nvidia 的正确财务框架，正在从"半导体周期"切换到"重资产工业周期"——而华尔街目前还在用错的尺子量它。

跨界类比：台积电之于晶圆，正在变成 Nvidia 之于"瓦特→token 的转化率"。前者卖制程，后者卖能量转化效率。

三、被所有人忽略的暗线：Cosmos 3 是对"互联网数据"的一次哲学性宣战

注意他讲 Cosmos 时说的那句话："互联网上所有的语言数据都是从'我们写、我们读'的视角，而机器人需要的是机器自己的视角。"

这是一句被快速带过、却在重写 AI 产业地图的话。它的含义是：

  1. OpenAI、Anthropic、Google 拿到的全部互联网语料，对 physical AI 几乎是无用的——因为视角错了。
  2. 第一人称视频数据（机器人、AR 眼镜、车载摄像头）才是下一个"原油"，而没有人拥有它。
  3. 这解释了为什么 Nvidia 把 Omniverse + Cosmos 当成生死线：如果真实世界数据稀缺，那就用模拟器合成第一人称数据。

跨领域的"啊哈时刻"：这与儿童认知心理学惊人一致——皮亚杰发现，孩子要到 7 岁左右才能脱离"自我中心视角"理解他人立场。当前的 LLM 类似于一个永远活在"第三人称叙述"里的成年人，它从未真正"具身"过。Cosmos 3 在做的，本质上是给 AI 补上人类婴儿在出生第一年完成的事——通过身体动作建立第一人称世界模型。

由此可推一个大胆预测：未来 24 个月，"第一人称数据"会成为继 GPU 之后第二个被疯抢的稀缺资源。拥有大量第一人称视频的公司——Meta（Ray-Ban）、特斯拉（FSD 车队）、字节（短视频原始素材）——可能在物理 AI 时代占据语言模型时代 OpenAI 的位置。Nvidia 不想等他们，所以自己用 Cosmos 造合成数据。

四、一个隐藏的系统性真相

把 Jensen 整场演讲压缩成一句话：他在告诉所有 CEO，公司的组织架构正在从"人类组织 + 工具"变成"agent 组织 + 人类作为最后一道审批"。

NVIDIA Open Shell 听起来像一个开源项目，但它的真正定位是：agent 时代的"企业操作系统 + 合规审计层"。Red Hat、Canonical、Microsoft 都站队——这是 1990 年代 Linux 出现以来，企业 IT 栈最大的一次重构。

最反直觉的推论：未来 5 年内，企业里"被裁员"的可能不是基层员工，而是中层经理——因为中层的核心职能（任务编排、信息路由、状态汇报）正是 agent harness 在做的事。Open Shell 的隐藏角色，是"中层管理者的 SaaS 替代品"。

Jensen 没说出口的那句话是：你买的不是 GPU，是一个比你公司还便宜的影子组织。

八、Vera CPU：为 Agent 时代设计的 CPU

Vera CPUs. CPUs built for the age of AI. All of the CPUs until now were created for people. We were the users. We were the users. We were the renters. Agents are impatient. They don't live in a world that is in seconds. They live in a world that's in nanconds. When it uses a tool, it wants the response time to be as fast as possible. When it access database, it has to come back as soon as possible. Every moment that the agent is waiting keeps it from going to the next step, the next step, the next step. But the one thing that is missing is we need a reference platform for robotic systems. These robotic systems are so complicated. So many motors, so many sensors, so fragile. And yet we need to have a way to deliver these reference platforms. Just like we do with PCs and DGXs and clouds and self-driving cars, we now are going to do it for robots.

Today we're announcing the NVIDIA Isaac Groot, a reference humanoid robot, all fully integrated. 25 degrees of freedom on the on each hand made by Sharpupa. 31 degrees of freedom on the robot. 6 feet 150 lbs. Just like me. The first number is shorter. The num second number is bigger. Otherwise, pretty close.

And and this platform runs the new Thor and our entire software stack, data generation stack, data simulation stack, the runtime, all integrated into a robot that is designed for everyone to use. Now, we built this for higher education and university researchers because for them to build this is in insanely hard to do.

十五、收官：一种计算模式贯穿一切

The computer industry has been completely changed in the last six months. Everything changed. Everything changed because agents were realized and it converged with the latest frontier models and it made possible the AI to now do useful work.

The computing pattern will repeat over and over and over again. This computing pattern of an agent that's a model, a harness that uses tools with skills and runs in a runtime. That runtime depends on whether it's in the cloud or on prem on a PC or in a robot. But the computing pattern is exactly the same for all of them.

You will use different harnesses because of your preference. You'll use different models because of your preference. You will improve them for your proprietary use. You would create sub super agents that you can rent to other people to help them do their work.

This agentic platform, this agentic pattern, Nvidia has an enterprise AI toolkit. This is a wonderful way for all of you to engage AIS and for us it's a wonderful growth opportunity.

Vera Rubin is in full production whereas Grace Blackwell was created to process AI particularly inference. Vera Rubin was created to run agents. It is in full production. It is much much more than a GPU. It is an entire disagregated distributed agent processing system.

Nvidia has really become an infrastructure company. Not just a GPU company, not just a systems company, but an infrastructure company to help you generate the maximum revenues, the maximum profit, and to get there as soon as possible.

The agent world, this new way of doing computing where you build CPUs now for agents, not for people, CPUs for agents, has its own special requirement. And our Nvidia Vera is revolutionary. I'm so happy about its ramp, the orders already. It's going to make it the fastest and the most successful product launch in our company's history.

Nvidia and Microsoft has created a whole new line of PCs. This is a new beginning. And of course, that exact same agentic pattern that I agentic processing pattern, computing pattern that I just described is also going to run on all kinds of devices. I mentioned PCs, but in the future, it'll be robots and satellites and base stations and factories in the cloud, on prim at the edge.

This pattern, agentic AI system, this agentic computing pattern will be replicated in computers all over. How we think about the personal computer will very likely change.

I want to thank all of you for your partnership, your friendship. We couldn't be here without everything that we do together. I am so proud of how you've been so successful this last year. The next year is going to be even more. Fore very optimistic. Fore Stanford. into the cut.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：黄仁勋悄悄宣布了"CPU 的物种灭绝"
表面上，整场演讲被解读为"Vera Rubin 上市 + 七大战略"。但真正的核弹被埋在一句话里：**"过去所有的 CPU 都是为人类设计的，Vera 是第一颗为 agent 设计的 CPU。"**
这意味着什么？过去 50 年，整个云计算的商业模式建立在一个假设之上——**人类用秒计时，按 vCPU core/小时租用**。AWS、Azure、阿里云的整个定价骨架都依赖"人感知不到的延迟 = 可压缩成本"。
但 agent 活在**纳秒世界**里。当一个 agent 等待 100ms，它就等于损失了一次工具调用、一次推理跳。Jensen 真正说的是：**云计算的计费单位即将从"core/hour"被迫切换到"token/watt"**——这是自 2006 年 EC2 诞生以来云行业最大的一次单位制崩塌。
讽刺的是，Intel 和 AMD 在这场演讲中一次都没被提到，但他们才是真正被掀桌的人。
## 二、跨领域连接：他正在把"计算机产业"重写成"工业革命的第三幕"
把演讲中三个看似无关的细节连起来： - **

item.title: [SemiAnalysis Weekly] Ep. 013 - AWS Margins Jump 10% While Azure and GCP Flatline (Tokenomics)
item.dc:creator: SemiAnalysis Weekly
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被低估的"角色反转"：xAI 从需求方变成了供应方

最让人震惊的不是 Anthropic 拿到 9650 亿美元估值，而是 SpaceX/xAI 把自己的 Colossus 集群租给了 Anthropic——也就是说，本应是 GPU 需求方的前沿实验室，悄悄变成了供给方。这在历史上有一个惊人的镜像：1990 年代 IBM 把自己卖不动的 PC 业务卖给联想，转身做服务和咨询。这不是失败，而是承认"前沿赛道我打不过你，但我账上的算力还能赚租金"。

Jeremy 用"giving up（放弃）"这个词形容这件事其实非常精准——但 Jordan 的反驳里藏着更深的洞见：算力本身是一种"看涨期权"。今天租给别人换现金流，明天有突破时随时收回。这种结构在金融衍生品里有个对应物：可转债（convertible bond）。xAI 其实是把自己的 GPU 写成了一张可赎回的可转债，租给 Anthropic 拿到收益，但保留了未来"转股"成训练资产的权利。这是 AI 时代第一次出现"算力的金融化分层"。

一个反直觉的会计真相：Token-as-a-Service 是唯一逃脱"产能扩张诅咒"的云业务

整个对话最大的技术洞察来自 Jeremy 关于"stabilized margin（稳态利润率）"的解释：CoreWeave 和 Oracle 的利润率不是因为业务差才低，而是因为他们还没"稳态"——数据中心还在折旧但还没产生收入。这种"J 曲线"在私募股权、生物医药 R&D、半导体晶圆厂建设中都极其常见。

但 AWS 做到了一件几乎反常识的事：在每季度新增一吉瓦容量的情况下，运营利润率反而扩张。这违反了传统重资产行业的所有规律。原因藏在一个微妙的会计差异里——Token-as-a-Service 的利润率上限是开放的，而 5 年 take-or-pay 合同的上限是被锁死的。当 Anthropic 的 ARR 在 3 个月里从 0 涨到 10B/月新增时，AWS 通过 revenue share 结构直接吃到了 Anthropic 增长的复利，而 CoreWeave 只能按合同拿固定租金。

跨领域类比：这就像 Netflix vs. 电影院线。电影院按座位定价，封顶就是上座率 100%；Netflix 按用户行为定价，理论无上限。Anthropic 是 AWS 的"流媒体爆款"，CoreWeave 们只租到了"放映厅"。

一个隐藏的市场分形：AI 市场在按"有上限 vs. 无上限"的任务边界裂变

Jeremy 引用了 Malcolm 的框架，但他没有展开的是一个跨越产业的深层结构：任何市场都会沿着"satiation point（饱足点）"分形。

  • 翻译：饱足点很低——再花钱也只是边际改进，对应"good enough"模型市场
  • 法律/科研/医疗/分析师：饱足点近似无限——多一份证据可能赢官司，多一个假设可能发论文

这跟米其林餐厅 vs. 快餐的分化、商务舱 vs. 经济舱的分化、奢侈品 vs. 快消品的分化是同一种数学结构。所以 Jeremy 说"如果你是第三第四名，你拿不到任何美元"在 open-ended 任务上是对的，但 Crystal 说"足够好就行"在 finite 任务上也是对的。真正的洞见是：他们都对，因为市场会沿着这条饱足线劈成两半，前沿模型吃掉无上限那一半（也就是绝大多数美元）。

而最反直觉的一个数学事实出现在他们的 Opus vs. Mythos 对比里：Mythos 每 token 贵 5–6 倍，但因为聪明 10 倍、所需 token 数少 10 倍，单任务总价反而更便宜。这是经济学罕见的"单位价格上涨但总价下降"现象——它的另一个出现场景是电动车 vs. 燃油车（每度电比每升油贵得多，但每公里成本更低）。AI 的"前沿溢价"本质上是智能密度的溢价，而不是 token 的溢价。

一个对所有"二流玩家"的残酷预言

最后留一个让人不舒服的观察：Anthropic 的崛起证明了一件事——AI 不是"水电煤"基础设施，AI 是"半导体光刻机"。水电煤是好用就行（commodity），ASML 是只有一家能造（winner-takes-all）。Meta 看到 Anthropic 后"hell yeah, double down"的反应，和 xAI 看到 Anthropic 后"算了我去当 neo cloud"的反应，本质上是两家公司对"AI 到底是什么形态的市场"做出了相反的判断。未来 18 个月里，至少有一家会被证明赌错了方向——而被证明错的那一家，将成为 AI 时代第一个真正意义上的"前 frontier lab"的化石。

📝 转录文本

Intro

Hello everyone, welcome back to SemiAnalysis weekly, episode number 13, lucky number 13. We're here with Joey, Jeremy, and Kristen.

We're going to talk about an article that we put out recently called "Anthropic growth and Bedrock mix drive AWS margins higher while peers lag." That means we're going to talk about everything Anthropic, including the recent announcement of their Series H, the release of Opus 4.8, but with the spin on focusing on infrastructure — like how exactly they serve these tokens, especially with their partnership with AWS.

Guys, welcome to the show. Excited to talk through this.

Thanks, Jordan. Man.

Bedrock and Token-as-a-Service Explained

All right, so let's dig in with the article itself and talk a little bit about the backstory. I think a lot of people understand the concept of tokens, understand what GPUs are, and certainly cloud providers and neo clouds, but not everybody is getting their tokens from the same place. So, can one of you guys give me a backstory on Bedrock? Like, what is AWS? What are they doing for Anthropic and how they're serving tokens with Bedrock? Joey, we'll start with you.

Yeah, yeah, yeah. I'll go through that. And I'll do it kind of for all the clouds as well.

So if we really look across all the hyperscalers, and especially the big three — when we look at Amazon, Microsoft, and Google — there's kind of two big breakouts, maybe even three.

There's a bit on the software side. So if you look at Microsoft like GitHub Copilot, that's an area that's like an AI software-as-a-service type product. They have AI infrastructure-as-a-service where they're just renting out these accelerator chips. And then they kind of have this token-as-a-service business, which is where they'll essentially expose these outside models or their own own models to consumers to interact with.

So it's a little bit of a different business because instead of just renting the underlying chip, you're renting the underlying model — buying it through your cloud, your CSP account and that enterprise spending agreement. You have all the same benefits around security, availability zones, and able to buy third-party models and also some of these first-party models through your cloud provider of choice.

So at a high level, those are kind of the three big buckets right now at the large hyperscalers that we see, and we're seeing some big changes or differences between them and how they've gone about it strategically. And the two main things was Anthropic's growth and Amazon's strategy around Bedrock has really driven their margins higher recently. That was really the takeaway from the article.

And this token-as-a-service is obviously much better business for the hyperscalers than infrastructure-as-a-service on the AI side.

AWS vs Azure vs GCP — Diverging Margins

Yeah, and if you look at the big three, they're kind of going in opposite directions right now in terms of their operating margin. That was the biggest chart from the article. I'll put it up on screen right now.

But Crystal, can you maybe explain — when we look at AWS versus Google and versus Microsoft and you try and break out the cloud business — is this all to blame on Anthropic from our perspective, or what's driving AWS to improve their operating margins while Microsoft's is declining and Google's kind of stays flat here?

So I feel like a lot of it is that cloud usage, right? A lot of people are using cloud more and it's mostly routing through AWS. And then, like what Joey was saying, the token-as-a-service business model — they just have a better margin on that as opposed to like the infrastructure-as-a-service model.

Makes sense, yeah.

How the Cloud Landscape Has Changed Since 2023

I think if you take a step back, the world of clouds has really changed a lot in 2023 as you started seeing these neo clouds, these GPU-as-a-service businesses. Because in the old days — which is basically 2022 and before — cloud service providers, you basically had three: Amazon, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure. They had amazing margins, easy returns on capital. Some new entrants like Oracle were trying to get in, but the market was dominated by three players that had an amazing business.

And now you get to GPU-as-a-service and what we first started to realize is that the barriers to entry are much lower. So Jordan, you're probably the best person to talk about this. You probably know the CEO of 150 neo clouds or 200 neo clouds. How many now? 300?

[laughter] Over 200. Yeah, yeah.

[laughter] It is pretty insane. Now obviously some are better than others, but the point is the market has much lower barriers to entry.

The moat that cloud used to be doesn't really exist in the AI era because it's really more about infrastructure. And especially the end users — the whole point of cloud computing was you make the IT much easier. Folks don't need to have such a big IT department internally. They can just rent through the cloud. It's super easy.

Now the big end users, they want to have much more control. You shift from platform-as-a-service in the old days to now bare metal. Folks want just the metal. OpenAI wants to think that the way they like it, Microsoft, Meta.

And so this is basically the token-as-a-service business is basically the first case at scale where you see an AI cloud provider having a business with a different profile. Right, because obviously as you have less of a moat in the GPU-as-a-service era, margins go down. Oracle — best example — they have an RPO of half a trillion dollars, which a few quarters ago, as of Q1, is still bigger than Amazon's.

[laughter]

Their backlog is bigger than Amazon's, but no one gives them the credit because people know it's much more risky, the returns are not the same.

And we've seen empirically that every single GPU-as-a-service cloud has faced struggles when they started to ramp up their business. But our view as a firm is we actually think that GPU-as-a-service business model is sound business. Companies like CoreWeave have a sound business, but there's this lag effect where as you ramp up and bring more capacity online, there's these lags that make your margins go down temporarily because your asset base depreciates, because you have to pay data center leases and so on.

And so it's really interesting to see that Amazon is basically the first cloud provider that, in a time of unprecedented capacity expansion — over a gigawatt per quarter now — they expand margins. And that kind of tells you that if at a period of accelerated capacity delivery they can expand margins, you start to think: okay, what's the stabilized margin of this business? And that's one of the points that we make — the stabilized margins of token-as-a-service for Amazon are actually extremely rich. The return on capital is fundamentally much more sound.

The Capacity Expansion Paradox

Yeah, let me throw this chart up on screen actually from the article, which is the percent of revenue that is going to Bedrock — Bedrock being the token-as-a-service at Amazon. You can obviously see it ramp up quite a bit at roughly the time that we're in right now, Q4 of last year and first quarter of this year. And then if we compare that to the chart I had on previously where we're seeing their operating margins improve in the first quarter of this year, they're saying that's due to it.

Can you explain maybe in more detail why it's unprecedented to say that Amazon can bring on a gigawatt per quarter and still improve operating margins?

So to understand this, you basically have to go back to: why are the pure bare-metal providers seeing their margins go down?

So you look at CoreWeave, which is a pure play — so they're the cleanest example. Oracle actually is kind of the same. When you look at this chart, essentially what this tells you is that their stabilized business does something like 25% operating margins, 30 to 40% gross margins.

But the whole issue is "stabilized" — stabilized means that your GPU cluster is fully functional, you're getting the monthly rent or whatever rent from your customer take-or-pay. So it's a flat fee, you know exactly how much revenue you're going to make. Oftentimes it's a 5-year take-or-pay contract, again fixed rate. So you know your revenue, you know your cost, everything is stable.

But before getting there, you obviously have to set up the data center, which is a huge capital expense up front. And then you have this whole process — or if you have the data center built but you need to fill it with equipment — that takes a few months. With some new stock types of equipment like GB200, which is super complicated, we've seen that lag get longer and longer. Which means that this period of time where you depreciate your assets, you pay data center rent, you pay some labor, you pay a whole bunch of stuff — that period of time goes up and you don't make any revenue because your cluster is not yet rolled out.

And so that's the whole dilemma that these guys are facing. They know their business model is stabilized structure, but they've been facing challenges to wrap it up. Some a bit contractual — again, GB200. Some just more structural because there is this lag.

And Amazon is the same thing. Like everyone else, they have the same profile. They're bringing on a whole lot of data centers, a whole lot of XPUs — these XPUs in theory should take time to bring online. Yet despite this, you're seeing their margins go up, which is a pretty good sign for them.

Yeah, and it's clearly different than the others in this space, where the percentage of their total AI revenue that they're reporting as being from token-as-a-service as opposed to other products is much higher than Google and Azure. Like, they are not just bringing on capacity, they're successfully selling it into the labs that are using it to serve tokens for these models.

Yeah, and I guess the chart you showed earlier — what I forgot to mention is obviously the margin buffer that you have when you sell tokens with your infrastructure, as opposed to having a 5-year take-or-pay fixed contract with a capped upside. So that's kind of one of the key points of the article.

Workload Mix — Why AWS Wins

Yeah, yeah. Guys, can you talk a little bit about the workload mix as well? Obviously any provider could conceptually do this, but not everybody is doing it successfully. It's not like Azure doesn't have a token-as-a-service business, or Google. In fact, even Crusoe, CoreWeave, Nebius are all trying to get into this business too. But they really need a customer, and they need a customer serving the right type of workload for it to really result in a bunch of growth, I would say.

Yeah, I would say for Amazon specifically, they benefit from the biggest customer base. They've been doing this for 20-plus years now at AWS. People are very comfortable buying that through them. People even buy infrastructure software through them — a provider like MongoDB, like Snowflake. Pretty large marketplace business. So customers are really comfortable with the security at this point, buying through them, having a single bill for all this.

And I think this comes back to some of the Anthropic news today — also on the ARR number of 47 billion. We look at Q1 and even in the Q2 here, a lot of Anthropic's business mix is much different.

So Amazon is benefiting from being 80 to 90% cloud in Bedrock, versus Microsoft is heavily OpenAI obviously. Google has a lot of Gemini, which doesn't benefit as much from a lot of these agentic coding tasks.

And when we look at the OpenAI or the coding percentage that's really driving Anthropic — you're talking probably like 10 billion a month net new ARR in the last 3 months, March, April, and May together. And a lot of that — probably 80% of Anthropic's net new ARR — is in this API business.

If we go to OpenAI, 60% of that business in Q1 was consumer subscriptions, really. So there's kind of a mix of factors. But Amazon was in the right place at the right time with Anthropic. And then two, they were able to give with Trainium 2 a pretty interesting deal structure for both parties. And I think that was the big thing too. We kind of mentioned in the article — their mix of Trainium, there's an infrastructure-as-a-service like fee component that Anthropic pays for this Bedrock infrastructure. But then there's some interesting hurdles around revenue share, and really margin share that happened. And because Anthropic was probably at 25 million of ARR per mid-one off the top of my head in Q1, versus like probably six back in Q2 Q4 — the numbers really made sense for both parties. And they really both benefited.

Forecasting Anthropic vs OpenAI

So, Eric — Crystal, can you talk a little bit about those forecasts you guys were making going into this end of Q1 and forecast for Q2? You don't necessarily get disclosures the same way from Anthropic, but at this point we've kind of been bang on with the disclosed revenue figures and the margin figures.

Mhm. I think for us, it's a little easier to forecast Anthropic than it was OpenAI, just because so much of Anthropic ARR comes from the API side. And because we also use Anthropic, and there's a lot of data out there about how people are using all of the different Claude models, it's so much easier to predict the workflow and kind of see what the token consumption is going to look like. And they've kept token pricing relatively stable these past two releases more or less.

Whereas with OpenAI, a lot of their revenue comes from these subscriptions and you never know if consumers are going to switch over to another one. So it's a lot harder to quantify the number of users that are using the subscription, when they can just cancel anytime, versus for API.

Opus 4.8 / 4.7 Release Notes

Yeah. Can you explain a little bit about the release on 4.8 and 4.7? Because pricing's remained the same but fast mode has changed, and there's been some other changes in terms of how they're doing pricing on the API.

Yeah, they said pricing is the same for regular, but then for fast mode it's different. Another cool thing that they said apparently was that it doesn't hallucinate as much, and they had like a pretty cool bar chart showing that its rate of hallucination is a lot lower for 4.8 and 4.7 supposedly. I haven't tested it out yet, and they said that it's super close to Mythos previews. So hopefully it'll stop just pulling numbers out of thin air when we're using it for our analyses.

That'd be good. That'd be good if numbers weren't pulled out of thin air. Yeah.

Yeah.

Coding Market and ROI in Token Spending

In terms of the fast mode and consumer subscriptions, you have comments there on what we've learned over the past few weeks or months from digging into how Anthropic is running their business on AWS — and what's maybe the biggest percentage of their revenue or their margin contribution across those different mixes, those different types of workflows that people could be consuming tokens on the API for?

Yeah, I mean, we have that in the tokenomics model. And we're doing a big study right now on what percentage geo coding market is of current token spend, especially on the API side.

We've done a lot of work too on just the consumer and to some on the B2B data of where Anthropic has been taking a ton of share of net new customers year-to-date, both in consumer subscriptions and enterprise.

But it's really difficult — and a big question among a lot of our clients: how big is the coding market currently? I think Anthropic recently said at their financial services day that financial services was the second biggest vertical. And we kind of know there's a pretty big gap between coding and financial services, in terms of especially API spend, of how people use this anecdotally.

And there's been a lot of recent comments too on token maxing, and especially the Fortune 500s — like, how do you budget for that? How do you blow through that spend over time? How do you measure ROI?

So things will have to change. People will put some processes in place. But I know guys like Jeremy get insane ROI at the data center model — and his team and him give that. And maybe there's less policing out there at some other organizations that just let people go crazy.

But that's another contributing factor I think — just the success of coding, and then too, Anthropic starting to win a lot of net new share on just the subscription side in both B2B and consumer, which we saw was really interesting in Q1.

The xAI / SpaceX Compute Deal

Yeah, I guess three things have happened since the last time we talked about this topic on the podcast.

First of all, they signed that massive deal with SpaceX AI Cursor, which added a ton of — Jeremy Wyatt laugh at over there.

This SpaceX AI Cursor — beautiful, beautiful.

Yeah, Cursor not part of it yet because they're trying not to change their S-1, I think. But anyway, the SpaceX S-1 revealed the many billions that Anthropic is spending with SpaceX, with the clause to let them back out — meaning xAI has the ability to reclaim these GPUs if they want. But that should be some contribution to Anthropic's total revenue. In other words, if they were constrained by compute for their ability to grow the business on the consumer subscription side and enforce rate limits or things like that, those should go away pretty quickly.

Series H — $965B Valuation

The second thing that happened is obviously that they raised their Series H — $65 billion in funding at a $965 billion valuation. So raising 65 billion at 900 billion and resulting in 965 post-money. I don't know why they didn't round that up to a nice even trillion, but we'll see.

Isn't that like almost double from February number, their valuation, right?

Yeah, what was the February number? They were at 400 something?

380 or 400, somewhere around there.

I guess they need to have their valuation track with their ARR growth.

I mean, it's not a crazy multiple, 20x — like versus the software bubble back in '21, some of those were like 80x, like Snowflake, Cloudflare. It's not super expensive. And then too, from a Wall Street Journal article recently — my financials that we also had in our model — they're profitable now. When you exclude stock-based compensation, Anthropic's a pretty good business model and they're seeing a ton of operating leverage.

And then we also know from the Information article we read today, OpenAI's not seeing that. And it's very clear what the better business is right now, and what the better business model is. There's obviously a lot of operating deleverage if people start cutting how much they're spending on coding tokens, but —

But we're not seeing that right now. We're not.

We're not. I mean, yeah, they're still growing at 10 billion net new ARR a month the last 3 months, which is pretty crazy. They're probably going to do another 10 in June. And who knows where they end up at the end of the year. With Mythos getting released as well, that probably helps. There's no train that's slowing right now. And they're in all the right places. They're seeing the benefits of that throughout the entire business model.

Bullish or Bearish — The xAI / Anthropic Debate

Big question here. xAI deal or SpaceX deal with Anthropic — is it bullish or bearish for the markets overall?

Which market? Neo cloud market or compute demand?

Overall — you know, everything's related. Stocks, compute demand, Nvidia, all of it.

I think it's super bullish. Neo clouds, man. Everybody wants to be a neo cloud. Neo cloud is the terminal business. Even AI labs want to be neo clouds, selling their compute to whoever they choose to right now.

Well, but I agree with this — because this deal is basically: one player that was supposed to be a source of demand becomes a source of supply. So now suddenly there's more competition in the supply market, which is GPUs-as-a-service, and the off-takers — there's one less. I don't know, man.

Yeah, I disagree with this. I think Cursor's training plenty of models on Colossus right now. I think they wouldn't have that provision in the contract to take back their GPUs if they really were going to be no source of demand in the future.

I think there's lots of demand to go around, and the reason that deal's happening is because Anthropic's demand is so overwhelming right now that they need to do these crazy things like buy compute from their competitors in order to be able to serve that demand. If they had a different way to be able to serve that demand, they would be doing it, I assume.

I mean, on the other hand, if you're xAI or Meta or whoever — other ones of these labs that are lagging — you're seeing Anthropic, you're like, "Whoa, bro, this is what I could do if I was at a frontier." If Grok suddenly was at the frontier, they could be a hundred-billion-dollar ARR business. So to some extent, you would argue this should make them more bullish.

I think they said xAI has about 24 trillion of enterprise AI applications carved out as future market TAM, right? So you don't even need to be a hundred billion ARR. You can just take that forward a few more quarters and take it to —

[laughter] 24 trillion, no problem.

So their TAM is 24 trillion, but that's for what? GenAI applications?

I'm going to bring up that chart now from the S-1. Yeah, it's like there's space and Telco, and then there's a really big section for enterprise AI applications.

Okay, enterprise AI applications. So that would basically be the TAM for Grok, right? And so they're saying actually we give up on the 24 trillion TAM. I'd rather give my compute to Anthropic. That's a better use case than fighting for a 24 trillion dollar TAM.

Compute Optionality vs Frontier Race

Well, I think in some ways it's just a matter of when you can spend that money — or when you can spend that compute. In other words, there's potentially some serial nature to the development of AI progress, where you have to wait for you or all of your competitors to run a bunch of experiments to figure out what the optimal model architecture and data set mix or just creation of data, synthetic data, whatever it is, is done.

Because if we were to just take a lot of these models that people are running today and try and run them on hardware three years ago — actually the model architectures would run really well. All the innovations in sparsity and attention — these are huge improvements over dense models from three years ago.

So they could have used that compute, if you're saying, "Hey, maybe they had a bottleneck because they weren't able to figure out new architectures that would have enabled them to use that compute efficiently." Then they could have used that compute to do research on these specific topics, right? Like less training, more research. Or they could have used it to give a whole lot of tokens to their employees and then make them much more productive at doing a bunch of research tasks.

Now we know that AI can do pretty complex science problems, right? The OpenAI math stuff. Which I know a few things about — [laughter] — because my dad does math for a living.

So yeah, I mean, I don't know. That does tell you that it's an odd decision when you're kind of in the fight and giving up, when you're seeing the strongest signals we've ever seen that this time is real and accelerating, and actually $10 billion of ARR per month. So [laughter] it's sad and it's pretty odd.

Because I feel like this is opposite to Meta. Like my sense is, to some extent xAI is giving up on the frontier race, whereas Meta is — if anything — getting more pulled up because of what they're seeing from Anthropic. They're like, "Hell yeah, this is what I bet on. I'm going to double down after having doubled down so many times already." So Meta is the one that is seeing Anthropic and being like, "Hell yeah." I want that.

Meta's Cash vs xAI's Venture Capital

Well, I — okay, I think there's two dynamics that you're overlooking here a little bit potentially.

One is that Meta has a huge cash-generating business that they can use to fund all of this. And SpaceX really just doesn't have this business that generates hundreds of billions of dollars of free cash flow that they can pour into compute for the research bets. So they have to do it based on venture capital, which is unfortunately finite when you're talking about the scale of tens or hundreds of billions of dollars and needs returns on some timeline — whereas Meta can do it on a longer timeline.

The second thing is, I think that the optionality of having access to compute that you can then take back and pour into something is actually quite powerful. Which is to say, if they're a cash-generating neo cloud business that can do some research on the side, and then in the future have some breakthrough or have some distribution moat with X or something in the Starlink or something in Tesla relationship — something that just means they can take advantage of it — they should in theory be able to then pour that compute into that thing that's just not ready yet.

Earnings Before Training — The EBT Framework

And I guess what I'm saying is — well, I really actually love Joey to cover a little bit about the "earnings before training" concept. Which is to say, other labs are spending a whole bunch of money training models right now that they need some return on. And right now they are getting them — namely Anthropic and OpenAI is getting a return on these models.

But Meta is getting no return on their models outside of the Rexis stuff. There's no return on Muse Spark, for example. xAI has very limited — they had like a million subscribers to Supergrok or something. It's quite different than approaching a billion miles for some of these consumer applications.

And so I think the play to say, "Well, Cursor is doing pretty well training on Kimi. Why don't we just let the open source guys build us a model for the next year, and then we'll take our compute back and go run a bunch with it, instead of spending a bunch right now just to keep being in fourth or fifth place" — is, well, I think it plays into the —

I have huge disagree.

Earnings-before-training argument, right?

Massive disagree. But we've been monopolizing the speech for a bit, so I'll let Joey jump in, but I have huge disagree here.

Well, you got to explain now after he says something.

The Frontier Premium — Is the Gap Widening?

[laughter] Yeah, sure. Well, look, it's pretty simple, man. What we're seeing right now is that there are tremendous returns to training compute. I think it's pretty clear. And you basically want to make sure you have more than others if you want to stay in the race.

Open source versus frontier — I think it's pretty clear that the gap is expanding, not closing. Which everyone was saying last year: open source is going to catch up, or the gap is going to close, China is getting closer. No, that's not happening. The frontier is — like beating to a massive extent the open-source models, as demonstrated by Anthropic. They are our trend. Like, I think anyone who does production or close-source sees the difference between Claude and Kimi or DeepSeek V4.

If you were to take a guess, would you imagine that the gap is going to expand or is going to narrow? I would assume that it's going to expand because one has much more compute than the other. Which goes back to the fundamental point: training compute has tremendous returns. And so not having compute means that you're disadvantaged relative to competitors.

I think we're agreeing about one thing — which is that training compute has massive returns if you're in first place, but not necessarily if you're in fifth place.

Not necessarily. And I think the point is — okay, let's imagine this. Let's say Meta goes completely crazy next year. They secure an amount of compute, so let's say they have 5x more training compute than Anthropic. Do you think they catch up? They're really far behind, but if they have 5x more compute than Anthropic, do you think they catch up?

No, probably not.

No?

No, obviously they need talent as well, but —

Yeah, please go ahead.

I think they need the talent. And so I do think there's a potential — I think you need both compute and the talent basically. And so I think that when xAI gives up all of their talent, or close to it with all the co-founders leaving, and then they give up all their compute, it kind of goes hand in hand there.

Yeah, I know — 100% agreed. But I think the point is, these two things tell you that they're basically out of the race. Then it's going to be incredibly tough for them to come back and extract value out of the $24 trillion enterprise AI applications market.

Back to the $22.7T TAM Chart

Okay, that came up again. So I'm going to pull that up on the S-1, and then Troy, maybe you can get us away from this argument and talk about the — yeah, this stuff here.

Here's their TAM. It wasn't 24, it was 22.7 trillion dedicated enterprise application. Little down here is Starlink.

[laughter] That is insane.

Anyway.

Does the Race Even Matter? Good Enough vs Open-Ended

Does the race even matter though? Because I feel like at a certain point, if Meta gets so much more compute and their model gets so much better — even if they're number three, number four, number five, or whatever — it's good enough for most people to use, right? And it's probably good enough to be replacing a lot of jobs already. That you don't have to be number one or number two to be winning.

No. No. No. No.

Okay, I think this kind of comes down to your perspective on how they actually use the models.

So like — I don't know. We're saying number three, four, five here, just to define it from my perspective (which others may disagree with): Anthropic's in first place right now because I believe coding is the only thing that matters. I think they've been proven correct. Coding is not "coding" — it's computer use, and everything a human can do with a computer and AI can do with a computer. And so therefore this is interface to the computer, not coding.

So Anthropic's in first, OpenAI's in second. I put Cursor in third. My experience using Composer is significantly better than using Gemini or using Muse Spark, because it's like you can't use it. And using the Grok models from xAI — and so in some ways, I think that Cursor has both the distribution and the model to be in third place right now.

And the question is just: how much compute does Cursor need to stay in the race? And I think that having the optionality to feed them more compute in the future is super compelling, whereas they can't use it right now. And so why have it on your balance sheet if you can't actually use it? Why not turn it into a revenue-generating asset and use it later once you have more distribution, or once you build out the training stack to improve it to the point where you can actually do these hero runs?

But we'll see — because there's four labs, five labs in the US testing this theory from different angles. Some are stacking compute and have a bunch of revenue, some are stacking compute and have no revenue, and some actually have quite a bit of revenue if you look at Cursor and don't have that much compute right now on a relative basis. And I'd like to see all three pursue it that way, because I'm not sure what the right playbook is or who the winner will be.

It's going to be interesting for Anthropic to attempt to defend their number one position, because that's not a position they've been in before. They've only had to play catch up. And I think that's actually quite hard. I think it's quite hard to retain talent. I think it's quite hard to keep pressing a compute advantage. I think it's quite hard to motivate users and consumers to keep consuming more, instead of getting — grass is always greener — some new feature from some competitor. And I think it's up to them to maintain a trillion-dollar market cap. We'll see.

EBITDA Before Training — The Investor Lens

This is — yeah, Jordan, I think it —

Jeremy, you want to go?

I said we've been talking a lot, man. I want other people to talk. But I had a response for Crystal — but you go first.

Joey: It goes into the earnings-before-training-interest-and-taxes concept. I think it's really interesting, like, we think of EBITDA — before training interest taxes — as the cash operating profits that they generate for running inference. And if you want to think of training and research as CapEx, you go back to this conversation — like, I think this is a big investor question, even corporate strategy question: that return on invested capital.

Right now we know Anthropic is obviously having massive returns on the invested capital they not only put in these models, but also just for coding applications, these computer applications specifically.

And then we're seeing more and more news of other hyperscalers — or not hyperscalers, AI labs — trying to get into this coding market. I think there was some Microsoft news this morning on that. Mixed opinions I think here of how successful that might be. But training more and more models for this — because obviously people do want to use frontier models to Jeremy's point.

We even saw the Meta token-maxing article — all that spend's external. Token spends external because of Jordan's computer and coding point. That's where there's a ton of product market fit, and they're seeing their own ROI when they use the product.

But yeah, that's heavy. I'm guessing Jordan's still on the call. So Jeremy, I'll send it back over to you.

Open-Ended vs Finite Tasks — Malcolm's Framework

Just wanted to respond to Crystal's point — because yeah, I mean, it obviously depends how you think the market evolves. But I really like the micro framework that Malcolm has, which is that — okay, I think 2030, 2035 — what type of tasks are going to drive the bulk of the total addressable market, the dollars that people are actually spending on AI?

And there's tasks that have a certain amount where they're good enough — that's kind of finite, like translation. Maybe at some point there's only time, it doesn't make any sense to spend more on translation. But then there's these very open-ended tasks where the spending is pretty much infinite.

On legal, for example, you could assume that if AI is really good at legal — if you want to make sure you beat your competitor, you probably want to spend more on AI than he does, have more intelligence than he does — because you want to gather more evidence, you want to think through many different ways of coming up with a defense and whatnot.

Scientific research is typically the very open-ended use case. Healthcare. For us as analysts, we try to get insights from gathering a lot of data — very open-ended. I would assume these use cases are going to drive much more spending than the finite sort of "it's good enough."

And really when you think of these open-ended use cases, what matters is to be able to do what you want to do in the cheapest way. And if you're doing it in the cheapest way, you're going to have to use frontier models. It's the whole point we made — Mythos is actually 2x cheaper than Opus. The model in terms of token pricing is 6 times, I think 5 times, more expensive than Opus. But if it's like 10 times smarter — if it requires 10x less tokens to answer a given task — then it's actually way cheaper to complete that task with the model.

And so I think at least that's the way I view it. And I think the bulk of the market is going to concentrate to the frontier models. I think if you're three or four or five, you're not going to get any dollars.

The 2026 Beat — Driven By One Company

And I think if you take a step back and think: what are the signals that we've seen in 2026? Did the AI market beat or has it missed versus expectations we had in 2025? Massive beat.

But then what I think is super interesting is: what is the composition of this beat? Did everyone beat, or is it just a few companies?

On the frontier side, it's basically one company. It's just Anthropic — massive beat. Google is probably tracking behind to some extent when you look at just the Gemini adoption and how much people were spending on Gemini. OpenAI is tracking behind. Obviously xAI, Meta are nowhere to be seen. You could have hoped that they would have had something. They don't really.

To be fair, on the open-source side, I think that it's also been a beat. I think there's been some good adoption, but the dollars spent are still pretty small. So I think it's an interesting composition of massive beat where it's basically all driven by one company. Which kind of gives you a sign that it's pretty much winner-takes-all. And if you're state of the art, you get the bulk of the volume. And if you're not, yeah, people don't spend on you.

I know Joey stopped playing. What do you think?

Can you repeat the last part of that?

Holy [ __ ], you didn't listen to my beautiful prose. I'll jump off.

I heard most of it. I got to jump off and respond to this.

Open-Source Startups — Encouraging but Small

So Jeremy, I think you — this totally makes sense. But we're also seeing massive beats or massive reported ARR numbers from startups serving open-source models right now. It's not just Anthropic growing. There's huge growth for Fireworks that's tied to Cursor.

Not that big, man.

Really big.

They're not that big, man. That is the thing. They're not that big. Some encouraging signals. I think Cursor is at what — 2 billion now? And they were at what, maybe 1.something at the end of 2025. There's still really good growth. Definitely you could say Cursor is a beat. I don't know Windsurf, I guess they're in Google, but they're worth to be seen.

Yeah.

No, I agree with you. Some of the open-source guys have had a beat, but in terms of dollar amount, still not very meaningful.

Yeah, I mean the claim from Fireworks is $315 million of ARR, right?

What is 300 million between friends? [laughter]

Okay. In the past, a startup unicorn was interesting when they were a billion-dollar valuation. Now they start to approach billion-dollars ARR, and you go "ah, whatever, fly on the wall."

Relative to the size of the market, which is already above 100 billion dollars — it's in the grand scheme of things not that big.

The Hyperscaler Positioning Recap

Think of the positioning of the different hyperscalers. I mean, we've talked about business models and whatnot, but the beauty of the economics 2.0 — this magic model is just so accurate, is that it covers —

Yeah, in 2 weeks we've gotten some pretty good feedback so far from some of these hyperscalers' customers. So it's been solid.

But right now, just given Amazon continues to win — I think the things that benefit them around their customer base benefit Azure pretty similarly. Especially as you look at more and more token-as-a-service type models that come on the Foundry.

And then obviously, Google — if they get a coding model. Right now when we look at what was formerly Vertex and is now Gemini Enterprise platform, we still think Gemini API is a pretty decent percentage of that. So we're not benefiting a ton from Claude and some of those things — but that's probably the biggest thing right now.

I mean, we still see Amazon — Bedrock token-as-a-service is pretty — I think by the end of the year it could be the majority of the AI business at Amazon. And even though Amazon, like AWS, lags Google (GCP) and Azure as their AI mix — at AWS, AI is a lot smaller percentage of the business — but with Bedrock going to the majority of the AI business, and then infrastructure-as-a-service being 80–90% at Azure and GCP, it's really really advantageous.

But then too, to that point, it's very easy for Azure to come in and add Claude as a model. This token-as-a-service business isn't that hard for these people with massive customer bases to implement. That's much harder as you go down to Oracle. And then to the neo clouds — to implement this at the same level, just given they don't have the massive inertia customer bases that really benefit from the old-school software distribution, enterprise software distribution moats.

The Long Tail Chart

Yeah, let me throw this chart on screen just to make the point Jeremy was making and you're making right now — which is, like, a bunch of rounding error for anything but the top three hyperscalers when it comes to the inference play business.

[laughter] Yeah, very small portion of the market that's going to ever go to the "everybody else" bucket.

And it also goes to the same point I was mentioning earlier with regards to the composition of the market. One of the important points he makes in that article is that the key to being a successful token-as-a-service business is actually just to have partnerships with the big labs and have access to frontier models.

It's pretty simple. So the big disadvantage that currently Nebius, CoreWeave, Iron, all of these guys have, is they don't yet have the partnership, and they also don't have maybe the capital to be able to deploy GPUs without a 5-year contract.

So it's kind of, as a function of the way the market works, that a company like CoreWeave or Nebius — maybe not Nebius actually, but Iron — have the bulk of their business contracted over multiple years. Whereas Amazon is free to be more spec, and better pick more demand that doesn't have that 5-year off-take, take-or-pay locked in.

Custom Silicon as a Margin Lever

Yeah, well — and that I think too, Jordan, on who wins is definitely the custom silicon portion. Around Trainium and TPUs at GCP — that I think is a big thing. Versus, like in the tokenomics model — and then when we look at brain accelerator and some of the data center model numbers — it's really at Azure mostly Nvidia. And then you look at that vertical integration at AWS and GCP — that's another big advantage for them, especially on the margin side I should say.

And then as we've seen a lot of inference get more efficient and gross margins on inference drastically improve at the labs — the two major frontier labs over the last two years — that's definitely been another key consideration when you think about who's going to win in this market.

Yeah. From the technical perspective, everything that we've criticized all the chip startups about, and TPU and Trainium, is always about usability. But if the entire chip — a gigawatt of Trainium at Rainier — is all just serving tokens from one to three models, well, the customer (the end-user customer) doesn't even necessarily need to know that much about what chip is running, if they're only buying tokens. And certainly not the end users — like the actual terminal user of the tokens. When I'm using Claude Code, I have no understanding of whether my token is coming from a TPU or a Trainium accelerator or a GPU. Doesn't make a difference, right?

Closing

Okay guys, well, really interesting stuff today. Anything left unsaid on the topic of Bedrock, tokens-as-a-service, Anthropic's growth?

Hey, I think we got it all, Jordan. Winners win, losers lose, and it was a clear trend.

[laughter] Winners win. Awesome. Okay, well, thanks guys for coming on the show. Good job today.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被低估的"角色反转"：xAI 从需求方变成了供应方
最让人震惊的不是 Anthropic 拿到 9650 亿美元估值，而是 **SpaceX/xAI 把自己的 Colossus 集群租给了 Anthropic**——也就是说，本应是 GPU 需求方的前沿实验室，悄悄变成了供给方。这在历史上有一个惊人的镜像：**1990 年代 IBM 把自己卖不动的 PC 业务卖给联想，转身做服务和咨询**。这不是失败，而是承认"前沿赛道我打不过你，但我账上的算力还能赚租金"。
Jeremy 用"giving up（放弃）"这个词形容这件事其实非常精准——但 Jordan 的反驳里藏着更深的洞见：**算力本身是一种"看涨期权"**。今天租给别人换现金流，明天有突破时随时收回。这种结构在金融衍生品里有个对应物：**可转债（convertible bond）**。xAI 其实是把自己的 GPU 写成了一张可赎回的可转债，租给 Anthropic 拿到收益，但保留了未来"转股"成训练资产的权利。这是 AI 时代第一次出现"算力的金融化分层"。
## 一个反直觉的会计真相：Token-as-a-

item.title: [Lex Clips] Where is antimatter hiding? - Physics explains the mystery of missing antimatter | Don Lincoln
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：我们整个宇宙是一个"会计四舍五入误差"

十亿分之一的不对称——每十亿个反物质粒子对应十亿零一个物质粒子——这个数字本身就是宇宙学里最荒诞的诗。你、星系、爱情、痛苦，全是大爆炸后那场惊天对消中"没消干净"的零头。这意味着宇宙的"主旋律"其实是湮灭，而存在只是噪声。换句话说，"存在"在统计意义上是异常值，不存在才是宇宙的默认态。这彻底颠覆了哲学中"为什么有物而非无"的传统提问方式——正确的问题或许是："为什么湮灭没有彻底完成它的工作？"

二、出人意料的跨领域连接：反物质生产 ≈ 风险投资的失败率

费米实验室每砸 10 万个质子才换回 1 个反质子——0.001% 的"成品率"。这个数字几乎精确对应硅谷顶级 VC 投出独角兽的命中率，也接近癌症免疫疗法中 CAR-T 细胞最终成功识别肿瘤的比例。任何一个需要"在巨大能量/资本/信息密度中筛出极稀有目标"的系统，都会收敛到类似的瓶颈结构：你无法靠优化通道效率突破这个比例，因为限制因素是"局部密度"而非"总量"——Don Lincoln 反复强调"是局部能量密度决定能否生成反物质，不是总能量"。

这条规律可以反过来诊断你自己的系统：如果你想产出稀有洞见、稀有作品、稀有关系，提升"总输入量"是无效的，必须提升"在质子大小的体积内的瞬时密度"。读一百本书不如把一本书压进一次深度对话。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：物理学家在用"无知"作为生产工具

Lincoln 最后那句"如果你不困惑，你就没在做你的工作"——这不是谦虚，而是一个职业方法论的反向定义。大多数行业把"困惑"视为待消除的故障状态；而前沿物理学把困惑视为唯一合法的工作面。一旦不困惑，研究者就被迫离开前沿，回到工程或教学。

这暗示一个被严重低估的系统性原理：困惑是一种稀缺资源，而非缺陷状态。AI 时代尤其讽刺——当所有人都在用大模型消灭不确定性时，真正的杠杆点反而是主动维持自己最优质的困惑。这与反物质的核心隐喻惊人对称：宇宙因为一个微小的不对称而存在；个体的认知突破，也只能诞生于一个被精心保护的、不被"答案"湮灭掉的微小困惑。

四、彩蛋洞察：反物质问题其实是个"会计学问题"，不是"物理学问题"

注意 Lincoln 是怎么"证明"不对称的——通过数光子和数质子。宇宙学家用的是宇宙微波背景里的光子数除以星系里的质子数，得出 10⁹ 这个比例。这本质上是宇宙级别的双式记账：湮灭产生的光子是"借方流水"，残留的质子是"贷方余额"，比值就是初始账目的不平衡。所以最深的物理学问题，最终落到了人类发明过的最古老的工具之一——复式记账。当物理学走到尽头，它会借用商人的语言。

📝 转录文本

一、从数学预言到实验确认：反物质的发现史

The virtual particles refer to matter and antimatter particles coming to life. — Correct. — Can we just talk about the antimatter part of that? So it's starting with Paul — one of the most legendary examples of math leading to physics. The math suggesting that something like antimatter should exist, and Paul Dirac taking it seriously, and then eventually showing that it does exist. So what evidence do we have for antimatter?

Antimatter was predicted in 1928. Paul Dirac was trying to merge quantum mechanics and relativity, because the original Schrödinger equation was not relativistic. In doing so — the equations were complex, but in the end it came down to something like equation² = 1. You take the square root of both sides, you get equation = +1 or −1. Plus one was the electron; minus one was something you didn't know what it was.

There was some conversation for a while — thought maybe it might be the proton, but that didn't seem to work out. And so he insisted that his equations were right, and that there was an antimatter — he didn't call it antimatter, but a positively charged sibling of the electron, what we now call the positron, the antimatter electron.

So it was predicted; it was discovered in 1932 by Carl Anderson and his student Seth Neddermeyer. They saw an antimatter electron, and that was pretty cool. Right there they knew it was real. Antimatter was predicted. It was observed. That's that.

In 1956, the antimatter proton was created — and that required a large particle accelerator, high enough energy to make it. That was done at Berkeley. A year later, the antimatter neutron was discovered.

二、从反质子到反氢原子：制造反物质的工程奇迹

So at this point — jumping ahead to now — we can make, using energy by smashing particles together, antimatter protons. We can make antimatter electrons. We have gone so far as to make antimatter helium nuclei. We have made two anti-protons and two anti-neutrons, combined them together to make an antimatter helium nucleus. This has been done, been observed. No question.

At CERN, they have gone so far as to make antimatter hydrogen. They take a beam off one of their lower-energy accelerators. They make antimatter protons. They collect them. They slow them down. They cool them to almost absolute zero. They take sodium-22, which makes antimatter electrons. They slow them down, bring them together, coalesce them, and they make literal antimatter hydrogen atoms — with an antimatter proton surrounded by an antimatter electron.

And they have done incredible measurements. They have agitated the atoms and caused them to emit light. They have looked at the light that comes out of antimatter atoms. The question is: does the light coming out of antimatter hydrogen atoms have exactly the same spectral characteristics as ordinary hydrogen — which we predict that it does? And the answer is: it does. So the tests have been staggering. We now know a great deal about antimatter hydrogen.

三、反物质会"向上掉"还是"向下掉"？ALPHA 实验

Recently — like 2023, I believe it was — one of the experiments called ALPHA at CERN made antimatter hydrogen, put it in a bottle, and released it, and watched which way it would go. Did it fall up, or did it fall down?

While it kind of makes sense maybe to think that antimatter falls up — in the same way that we have Coulomb's law, you've got electric charges and they might attract or repel — however, there were lots of ample theoretical reasons to believe that antimatter also would fall down.

So they did this fantastic measurement. First they put in hydrogen and they calculated that something like 80% of the hydrogen atoms would fall through the bottom of the bottle and 20% would go through the top — just because gravity is very weak and the atoms will escape wherever they do, but there will be a bias pulling hydrogen atoms down.

They did exactly the same thing with antimatter — and what did they find? They find that antimatter falls down. Now, they do not have a good enough measurement at this time to say that the gravity that antimatter experiences is 100% that of matter. What they have measured is that antimatter fell down with 75% the strength of regular matter. But there were big uncertainties — plus or minus .13 due to the experiment, which was good but imperfect, and plus or minus .16 due to their theoretical model. So it's like 75 plus or minus something like 29 — and that means there's a good chance it's between .5 and 1, which means it's consistent with one. So they are improving their measurements.

四、制造反物质有多难？费米实验室的数字

Well, if I can, I would love to take a bit of a tangent on that topic, because I went down a rabbit hole watching some of your videos on antimatter. Fermilab was the hub for the production of antimatter for quite a while. — It was. — I saw that NASA said that the global estimate for the current rate of production of antimatter is 1 nanogram per year. Can you speak to how hard was it to make antimatter? And also you did mention in a video that if matter and antimatter meet, they produce a lot of energy. I think 20 grams of antimatter is equivalent to a 1-megaton nuclear warhead in terms of explosive energy. So all those questions together — how hard is it to produce antimatter? — It's freaking hard.

Okay. All right. So here's the deal. At the time — until 2011 — Fermilab was the most powerful anti-proton production facility on the planet. Every 2.3 seconds, we would smash 10¹³ protons into a target and we would get out 10⁸ anti-protons. So basically, in order to get a single anti-proton, we needed to smash 100,000 protons into material.

Every 2.3 seconds we would get on the order of 10⁸ antiprotons. What we would do is collect them over the course of 12 hours or so. We would have to collect them and cool them down and so forth — on the order of 10¹² anti-protons every 12 to 24 hours. 10¹² sounds like a lot. It really does. That is a trillion.

But you need to remember that a gram of antimatter is 10²³ antiprotons. So that means over the course of a day we were able to create something like 100-billionth of a gram. If we did that for a year, that would be about a nanogram. So about a nanogram a year, give or take. That's a reasonable estimate. A nanogram — one-billionth of a gram.

So at that rate, with that facility, it would take a billion years running with very little downtime to make a single gram of antimatter. If you combine 1 g of antimatter and 1 g of matter together, the energy release is equivalent to the combined Hiroshima and Nagasaki explosions. So that tells you — if you wanted a megaton, you need about 25 times more. So you would have to run for 25 billion years to get a megaton of explosive power.

五、$1.5 千万亿美元的炸弹：反物质的经济学

Let me lay it all out, because I think it's pretty interesting actually. This is a NASA estimate of how much it costs to produce antimatter. Looking at the cost of the accelerator, everything combined to do enough for a 1-megaton antimatter bomb — if such a thing would even be possible — on the order of 25 grams, would cost, based on the NASA estimate, about $1.5 quadrillion. By the way, NASA wasn't talking about a bomb — that's just me adding. NASA was talking about the estimate of $62 to $63 trillion per gram of antihydrogen, actually, is what they're referring to.

So compared to current best estimates of how much it takes to produce a 1-megaton nuclear warhead — everything combined is about $10 to $50 million in the United States. So you're talking about a difference, in terms of a weapon with equal power: $50 million versus $1.5 quadrillion.

六、反物质推进与星际旅行的诱惑

To me, what's interesting — weapons is just one indication of this. One other possibility, and NASA also writes about this, is the use of antimatter in propulsion systems — just like you can use nuclear fusion, and maybe even nuclear fusion down the line, in propulsion systems. I saw that one gram can help get us to the Alpha Centauri star system — if we can get to 0.2 times the speed of light — in 20 years, meaning it would take us 20 years to get to Alpha Centauri.

Is any of this a possible future? The use of antimatter for generation of energy — because we should mention that it's extremely compact. It has the obvious downsides that it's extremely costly to produce; we don't know how to do that kind of scale. The upside is it's compact, very powerful.

So the short answer is: it is not a physics problem. It's an engineering problem. So I have people for that. Okay. Um, okay. But — the truth is that antimatter, if you are able to assemble it and store it, sure, it would be able to take that antimatter, heat up matter and shoot it out the back of a rocket, and it would do what rockets do, and it would make us go quick, and that would be fine.

And we should mention — the thing that you just mentioned is correct. One of the hugest challenges is the containment.

— Because antimatter, when it comes in contact with matter, is a problem. — Right. So if you were unable to contain your trip to Alpha Centauri for even a millionth of a second — boom. And that would not be good. — Yeah.

You know, it reminds me of Star Trek where Scotty's saying, "Captain, the antimatter pods — we're losing containment, going to blow." That's exactly what would happen.

So the short answer is yes, antimatter as in principle we could make and use as a source of energy, but there are probably far less expensive sources of energy. It depends on what you need to do. The Voyager probes are still chugging along with plutonium. They're running out of energy at this point, but we could presumably do a somewhat better job if we needed to.

So I like the idea of antimatter, but the reality is the danger — not the obvious danger of weapons, but the danger of: if you wanted to be in a ship run by antimatter, if it ever got loose, well, you would never know it. That would be that.

七、突破口在哪里？答案：能量密度

The reason I find this kind of inspiring is — antimatter is in the space of physics that has a lot of mysteries. There's a lot of exploration to be done. And this kind of connection to energy means that if we have a bunch of breakthroughs on the antimatter side, that might lead to a better propulsion system, better energy generation systems. — In principle. — There's some combination of engineering here, but there's some combination of understanding the fundamental physics. — I mean, we know how to do this. You take energy, you make antimatter. You have to contain it, you have to store it, you have to do all the hard things. But I would be shocked if there was some like new addition to the theory that made antimatter production easier. — Interesting. So we know how to produce antimatter with accelerators. You're saying there's no breakthrough in physics that could lead to different mechanisms for the generation of antimatter? — You have to concentrate energy. That's it. If there's another way to concentrate energy, that would work too. — And our best knowledge of how to concentrate energy is the accelerator.

And remember — we're talking concentrating it into volumes the size of a proton. If you concentrate it to the size of your thumb, well then, it's really the density that matters, the local density. When you smash two protons together, all of that's occurring in a tiny tiny volume. So it's the local density of energy that matters. If you had a lot of energy in a thimble or something, it's probably not dense enough. It really has to be in close proximity for that to happen.

So if there's another way — we know how to do it, to make that density thing with accelerators. If someone has a bright idea on how to make highly dense energy, then yeah, making antimatter is a piece of cake. But that's the crux: concentrated energy.

— Yeah, and how to do so in a cost-efficient manner. Not trillions of dollars.

八、最大的谜：反物质都去哪儿了？

Well yeah — so one of the big mysteries with antimatter is the bigger why. Where is the antimatter that should kind of be there? If the whole idea is that anytime you generate matter, you generate the same amount of antimatter — and yet, when we look out into the observable universe, it seems like there's not antimatter for the most part there. Correct? So what do we understand about this mystery? What are the possible explanations as to why?

There's this thing called baryogenesis. And, reiterating a little bit what you just said — these are both Einstein things. Einstein says that when you take energy you make matter and antimatter in equal quantities. And Einstein says: after the Big Bang there was a lot of energy in the universe, which should have made matter and antimatter. We only see matter. Where'd the antimatter go? And the answer is: we don't know.

However, there are some ideas. And there's a lot of thinking on it. In fact, Fermilab is doing an experiment right now with neutrinos, trying to better understand what it was that made the matter and antimatter not be the same.

Now, we do have a measurement of how much different it should be. And it's kind of neat. We can do this by counting the number of protons in the universe — just looking at galaxies and so forth. And then we can look at the cosmic microwave background, which is sort of the aftermath of the Big Bang. And we can count the number of photons from the cosmic microwave background. With a little bit of math, we can then say that somehow in the early universe, something made a very very tiny asymmetry:

For every billion-billion — with a B — antimatter particles that existed in the universe, there were a billion and one matter particles. The billions cancelled, annihilated, destroyed each other — and that extra one that's left over is us.

So what physics mechanism made that ever-so-slight asymmetry is not understood. There are some thoughts.

One thought is that — well, it's just how it was when the universe was formed. There was an asymmetry. It was not made by matter or antimatter.

Another possibility is — there are various numbers of theories all under the word baryogenesis. Baryo coming from the word baryon, which basically means protons; and genesis meaning the creation of. We'd say that simply because the protons are the heaviest particles, so baryogenesis is just the creation of matter. There are a number of theories in quantum mechanics that say that matter and antimatter can oscillate back and forth into one another. And there is a slight asymmetry in how that happens. We know that this is true to a degree — we've measured it in the 1960s with a different form of matter. Not protons, but a type of ephemeral matter that only exists in particle accelerators. So we know that there is a slight difference between matter and antimatter, but it's not enough. It doesn't explain that. We're not sure.

九、轻子起源：费米实验室押注的颠覆性假说

So at Fermilab, we have this idea which kind of turns things on its head. It's not baryogenesis, it's leptogenesis. Leptons are the electrons — and because Fermilab is currently the world's most powerful neutrino accelerator, and neutrinos are leptons, there is this idea.

Now, leptogenesis is incredibly complicated, but the idea is: it is possible — we know that neutrinos actually change their identity. There are three different types of neutrinos, like cats and jaguars and tigers. If you have a beam of just cats, if you go along a little while, you find there's cats and jaguars; and then tigers; and then they'll be back to all cats again. This oscillation thing is called neutrino oscillation. We've known it's been true since 1998.

What we are studying is: we're going to make a beam of neutrinos and another beam of antimatter neutrinos. We're going to study the oscillation behavior of the two of them. And it is possible — it is unlikely, but it is possible — that the two of them will oscillate at slightly different rates. And if the neutrinos oscillate at slightly different rates, then that, along with several other highly improbable things, can tie together and might explain why there is more matter in the universe.

If I was going to bet the farm, I'll bet that they oscillate at the same rate. But I don't know, and you don't know — till you do the measurements. So that's what we're doing. There are some other experiments trying to measure it right now. So there's a big race between the Fermilab group and another group in Japan to see who gets there first and makes this measurement, and we will find out.

If it turns out though that there is a difference in this oscillation rate between matter and antimatter, it will be a huge clue in this very very difficult puzzle. I wish I could tell you I knew what the answer is — but literally nobody knows. And that's the thing of being a research scientist like me: if you're not confused, you're not doing your job.

十、收束：我们都是一个"幸运的会计误差"

— So, there is this desperate — or not desperate, exciting — search for this tiny asymmetry. — Yes. — It's so crazy to think that everything we see around us is a result of this tiny asymmetry. That there was this gigantic annihilation of matter and antimatter in the early universe — and this is just some little accident. — Yeah. Yeah. That's crazy. — It's a happy accident. That is just — I mean, it's totally crazy.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：我们整个宇宙是一个"会计四舍五入误差"
十亿分之一的不对称——每十亿个反物质粒子对应十亿零一个物质粒子——这个数字本身就是宇宙学里最荒诞的诗。**你、星系、爱情、痛苦，全是大爆炸后那场惊天对消中"没消干净"的零头**。这意味着宇宙的"主旋律"其实是湮灭，而存在只是噪声。换句话说，**"存在"在统计意义上是异常值**，不存在才是宇宙的默认态。这彻底颠覆了哲学中"为什么有物而非无"的传统提问方式——正确的问题或许是："为什么湮灭没有彻底完成它的工作？"
## 二、出人意料的跨领域连接：反物质生产 ≈ 风险投资的失败率
费米实验室每砸 10 万个质子才换回 1 个反质子——**0.001% 的"成品率"**。这个数字几乎精确对应硅谷顶级 VC 投出独角兽的命中率，也接近癌症免疫疗法中 CAR-T 细胞最终成功识别肿瘤的比例。**任何一个需要"在巨大能量/资本/信息密度中筛出极稀有目标"的系统，都会收敛到类似的瓶颈结构**：你无法靠优化通道效率突破这个比例，因为限制因素是"局部密度"而非"总量"——Don Lincoln 反复强调"是局部能量密度决定能否生成反物

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Hidden Problem With MCTS – Eric Jang
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、MCTS 的"伪保证"——一个被普遍误解的训练神话

表面上，MCTS 被描述为"严格优于策略网络的改进算子"，但 Eric 在这段对话里悄悄揭开了一个被工程师们集体回避的事实：MCTS 的优越性不是数学定理，而是一种工程信仰。它的"保证"建立在两个前提上——价值网络足够准确、模拟次数趋于无穷。一旦任何一个前提失守，MCTS 不仅不能改进策略，反而会主动污染它。

这里有一个令人意外的观察：MCTS 的失败模式不是噪声，而是系统性偏见的放大器。当训练数据缺失某类局面（例如对局都在中盘投降），叶子节点的价值评估会系统性失真，而 MCTS 的反向传播机制会把这种失真沿着搜索树向上累加并放大。换句话说，搜索越深，错误越被"权威化"。这就是为什么一个看起来"更聪明"的算法会让模型集体退化——它把局部的盲区，编织成了全局的幻觉。

二、跨领域连接：MCTS、Dagger、自动驾驶与"幸存者偏差"的共同骨架

Eric 自己提到了 Dagger 和自动驾驶的偏离纠正，但更深的联系在于：这三者本质上都在对抗同一种结构性病——分布外状态的评估真空。 - MCTS 因为对局总在中段结束，失去了对终局的"肌肉记忆"； - 模仿学习 因为专家从不进入危险状态，学不会从危险中恢复； - 自动驾驶 因为路测数据偏向正常道路，对极端工况盲目自信。

把它们并排看，会浮现一个意外的洞察：这其实是"幸存者偏差"在强化学习中的算法形态。亚伯拉罕·瓦尔德当年在二战飞机上发现：你看到的弹孔分布，恰恰是"没被打下来的飞机"留下的，真正致命的位置反而没有数据。MCTS 的训练 buffer 正是如此——它积累的全是"被认为重要的对局轨迹"，而那些被资源效率优化掉的"非重要片段"，恰恰是模型未来崩溃的位置。Eric 提出的"10% 的对局打到终局"其实就是机器学习版的瓦尔德修正：故意收集那些"理应不重要"的数据，因为重要性本身就是一种偏见。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：所有"自我改进"算法都共享同一个脆弱性

如果把视角再拉高一层，会发现一个更深的模式：任何依赖"自己评估自己"的系统，都内嵌着同一类崩溃几何——AlphaZero 的自我对弈、LLM 的 RLHF、人类的自我反思，乃至组织的 KPI 体系，全都遵循同一条曲线：评估器的盲区会通过反馈循环被放大成系统的核心信念。

这里有一个反直觉的推论："提早投降"这种看似理性的效率优化，恰恰是 AlphaGo 体系最危险的设计选择。它在短期节省算力，却在长期掏空模型的"终局直觉"。这种现象在人类组织里有一个对应物——那些过早结束的项目、过早放弃的对话、过早裁定的失败，构成了组织永远学不会的盲区。所谓"知道何时止损"的智慧，从信息论角度看，其实是一种主动制造分布外灾难的机制。

所以 Eric 那个"让 10% 的游戏打到底"的工程 trick，其实暗含一条普适法则：任何自我改进系统，都必须强制保留一部分"看似无意义的完整经历"，否则它终将在自己最自信的地方崩盘。这条法则同时适用于围棋引擎、语言模型、科研团队，以及——你自己的学习方式。

📝 转录文本

MCTS 的核心机制与 Dagger 的类比

What MCTS is doing is basically saying like you play this game where you eventually lost, but on every single action, I'm going to give you a strictly better action that you should take instead. It does not guarantee that you are going to win, but it does guarantee that, you know, if you take these tuples as training data, so that you retrain your uh your your policy network to predict these ones instead of these ones, you're going to do better.

And uh this is very related to dagger in robotics and in imitation learning where you want to collect a intervention here. And even if you're in a in a not great state, for example, like a self-driving car that, you know, veers off the side of the road, there is still a valid action that kind of corrects you and brings you back.

提问：MCTS 一定优于策略网络吗？

Yeah. Okay, so um pedantic question. But is there a guarantee that MCTS must be better than the policy? For example, you could imagine early on in training, because MCTS is informed by the value network,

Yeah.

early on in training, uh when the value network hasn't been well trained on finished uh games, um that like MCTS is worse than sort of randomly initialized policy. So, is it just like a heuristic that MCTS is better than the policy, or is that like is there some guarantee?

回答：这只是启发式，不是保证

Right. In in practice, it is a heuristic, um and it does work in also in practice, but let me illustrate an example where MCTS can give you a worse distribution than your policy network.

So, um and this can often happen if your self-play algorithm um has trained to a good point, but then somehow it's uh it collapses because it's um it's not trained on diverse data or something, right?

失败案例：终局价值评估的崩塌

So, um let's say we have a board state where um the policy recommendations here are very good. So, so like, you know, pi of AS is like great. But, um somehow, because maybe we're playing on a lot of games where the the bots just resign instead of playing all the way to the Trump Taylor resolution, they kind of forget um how to evaluate those kind of late stage plans, right?

Like in the in the case that we showed with the corner play, maybe like 100% of our training data in our replay buffer has lost examples of how to evaluate the value function at those states. So, you might end up in a scenario where your terminal value is like very bad. And if the terminal values of the leaves are not good, then this will actually propagate all the way up and cause your your your puck selection criteria and your backups to be off. And then you're end up visiting a very very different distribution than what your policy initially recommended.

模拟次数与方差问题

Um also, if your number of sims is low, then you might also have a variance issue where you just don't explore enough, right? Like like a it's only guaranteed to converge when you kind of take end to infinity. Um So so variance in, you know, your search process as well as inaccuracies in your evaluation can definitely screw with the quality of your policy network recommendation. And so that's why it's not a guarantee to improve.

AlphaGo Lee 的工程对策

But and and that is why I think I I suspect why AlphaGo Lee had the playouts to the end in their training algorithm so that they could ground this thing in real playouts.

Yeah.

Um in practice, what you could also do is just like for 10% of the games, you you prevent the bots from resigning and you just say like resolve it to the end. So you get some training data in your replay buffer to really resolve those kind of like late stage playouts that normal human players would would kind of not play to.

Yeah, yeah. If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
