这就回到了最开始那个悖论——整个市场上最珍贵的东西，往往是看起来最不性感的那些。 TSMC、Broadridge、Micron 的供应链关系，乃至日本人对办公桌的那种荒诞的虔诚——它们的共同点是：都很难被一句话讲清楚，所以也都很难被一句话替代。

📝 转录文本

开场：TSMC 的估值之谜

John Wu： TSMC is historically a low ball, very steady, high quality company, but relative to other AI stocks, it's still very cheap, especially.

Bonnie： You just said it is still not as expensive.

John： If I had a portfolio, I would definitely put some money, if not a higher waiting into TSMC.

文化片段：地震来时，工程师先回公司

John： Whenever there's an earthquake, all the engineers, they don't go to their kids or their wives. They head straight back to their companies.

Bonnie： Just Taiwan. Are you talking Taiwan? Taiwan.

John： But it's not like Americans don't work hard.

川普的目标：50% 芯片美国制造

Bonnie： President Trump said, "We want 50% of chips made in the USA."

David： How long can the company sustain its technological edge? Because that's the thesis here, that nobody can do what they do, at least for now.

嘉宾介绍

David： 很高兴再次欢迎 John Wu 来到 Consensus Miami 的节目。John 是 Ava Labs 的总裁，加入 Ava Labs 之前，他在华尔街做了多年的科技投资人，管理过自己的基金，更早之前是 Kingdom Capital 的投资组合经理。我们很高兴邀请他来谈谈科技投资和区块链投资的未来。欢迎来到节目，John。

John： David, it's pleasure. Always nice to see you.

David： 今天和我一起的还有 Bonnie。

Bonnie： Bonnie Chang. 镜头之外我们刚才聊到 TSMC，所以我是一个超大的投资者——不是超大的投资者，而是我投资组合的超大部分。

David： Whale. TSMC whale right here.

Bonnie： 因为我持有它最久，对它非常 passionate。所以你刚才说它还不算贵？

为什么 TSMC 还不算贵？

John： 我认识投资 TSMC 的人。正如 David 所说，我做了很久的专业科技投资人，我认识的不只是专业对冲基金投资者、风险投资人投资这些东西，还有这些公司的运营者。

TSMC historically a low-vol steady high-quality company，估值通常相对自身是非常合理的。它可能比自己历史上贵一些，因为它处在如此重要的位置，而且他们做的事情是最好的。但 relative to other AI stocks, it's still very cheap，尤其考虑到增长率。

Bonnie： 是因为地理位置吗？

John： 我觉得地理位置有一点折价，正如我们在镜头外聊到的，因为它靠近中国。但我觉得总体上，人们对这家公司的认知不如它是美国公司时那么深。美国公司天然会获得更多曝光。

Bonnie： 所以如果我有同样的钱，两家同等的公司，你会选择美国市场吗？

投资 vs 跟随：免责声明

John： 好，我要先做个免责声明。我以前是投资人，现在是一个 passionate follower，我有还在投资的朋友，我也持续关注这个领域。所以这不是投资建议。

如果我有投资组合，我肯定会把一部分钱，甚至更高比例放在 TSMC 上。而且不只是 TSMC，台湾还有很多 AI 供应链上的相关公司，韩国有很多内存公司，日本有 Tokyo Electron。亚洲在 AI 的硬件和电子工程层非常擅长，而这正是很多稀缺资源所在。 所以亚洲有很多好东西可以看——我应该说是亚洲的公司——而 TSMC 大概位于这个名单的最顶端。

美国政府入股 Intel：另一个变量？

David： 但你会不会押注美国公司被政府收购或至少投资，就像 Intel 那样，几个交易日就涨了两位数？我不是说一定会发生，但比较一下东方和西方，这是不是一个要考虑的因素？

John： 作为一个美国人，我希望 Intel 能在它的代工业务上建立 sophistication，在美国创造就业。但他们还有很远的距离。Intel 在做 CPU 上也很厉害。Intel 之所以上涨，不只是因为管理优秀，也不只是因为他们想在美国创造代工层级的成功，还因为 with the Agentic World coming, there's a scarcity in CPUs。

所以你会因为各种原因持有 Intel，不只是因为美国持股。但这一点也不坏。

在海湖庄园：川普的 50% 承诺

Bonnie： 几周前我去了 Mar-a-Lago，川普总统说："We want 50% of chips made in the USA."

John： 美国总统希望就业和制造业回到美国，没有任何问题，我觉得这是好事。他一直坚持这个主题，他愿意支持美国公司。就像我刚才对 David 说的，这不是坏事。

Bonnie： 但我想知道你的立场——这有可能吗？

John： 哪种意义上的可能？

文化作为护城河：地震、过劳与工作伦理

Bonnie： 在亚洲，地震发生时，工程师们不会回家找孩子或妻子，他们直接回公司。

John： 只是台湾吗？你在说——

Bonnie： 台湾。第一部分。

John： 这种情况一直都有。日本有一个词，专门指"在办公桌上因为过劳而死"，这其实被认为是一种非常高贵的死法，相对于被公交车撞死。

Bonnie： 我不知道这个，是吗？

John： 有这个词，相信我。每个人都欣赏亚洲社会和文化的辛勤工作伦理，但美国人也不是不努力工作。

美国人有一种略微不同的文化，他们有非常 entrepreneurial 的文化，他们更愿意冒险。所以如果你能——当我们在创立 Ava Labs 的时候，最初十个人在房间里，全是 Cornell 的工程师，很多人是亚裔，工作伦理很强，但这并不重要，我们都很努力，同时我们也很有创业精神。如果你能把这两件事融合在一起，那就是理想场景。

TSMC 还能领先多久？

David： 在我们继续之前，我想问你们两个一个关于 TSMC 的问题：这家公司还能保持多久的技术领先？因为这就是论点所在——没有人能做他们做的事情，至少现在没有。

John： 不幸的是，当我开始投资科技的时候，科技周期更长，而经济周期更短。今天 2026 年我们生活的科技世界的现实是：持续不断的创新可以成为优势，也是唯一的优势。

即使在量化交易公司，这些量化模型的半衰期越来越短。这就是为什么这些公司有一大堆 PhD，因为新模型必须不断推出。It's constant innovation. 最终它会为社会带来好结果（希望如此），但工作的人会非常辛苦，必须不断思考新东西。

投资组合设计：个人 vs 专业

Bonnie： 我很欣赏这个洞见。你现在会如何设计你的投资组合？我们说的是 AI、能源、防御性股票吗？

John： 会有——听着，如果我还坐在专业的位置上，有专业的投资组合，也有个人的投资组合，比如一个有日常工作的人。

Bonnie： 先说个人的。

John： 好，我觉得这是一个超级激动人心的时代，让我想起互联网时代。如果你能找到一个投资组合——你会有一些押错——但如果你能找到当年的 Amazon、Google 和 NVIDIA，作为个人投资者，你不必应付日常波动。

如果你能找到 AI 世界未来各个部分中最好的潜力股的组合，你就坐下来，持有一段时间。 这是一个伟大而激动人心的时代。想象一下 25 年前，你可能会押错几个，但如果你识别出 Google、Netflix 和 Amazon，就持有它们，不要担心起伏。

专业投资组合

Bonnie： 谈谈专业的投资组合。

John： 现在的专业投资组合人不可思议。说说自由裁量投资组合，不是量化。他们非常擅长识别每一个瓶颈，这就是为什么你突然之间从只看 NVIDIA，转到看网络——不只是网络，还有 photonics——然后是内存，比如 Micron、韩国的 SK Hynix。他们一直在寻找下一个瓶颈，提前布局。这就是为什么你会看到这些超级飙升。

Micron 是一个完美的例子。 Micron 本来应该是一个周期性的、缓慢的内存业务，按 PE 算还很便宜，但人们不知道它能持续多久。然而这只股票看起来应该是按"无盈利"来交易的，但它现在交易在不到 10 倍明年预期盈利。

Reddit、ETF 与主动管理的消亡

Bonnie： 我听说 Micron 是从 Reddit 开始的。

John： 好，你进入了一个完全不同的领域。这件事好坏参半，我很纠结。

当我还是专业投资人的时候，人们更强调估值，更强调主动管理。大家相信他们真的能创造 alpha，纳斯达克和纽交所交易量的很大一部分都是主动管理者——共同基金或对冲基金。

今天，真的是量化驱动、ETF 驱动。 ETF 现在占据了流量的很大比例。所以股票比以往任何时候都更高度相关，一起涨一起跌，有时候和股票本身没关系，仅仅因为 ETF 的权重。当出现安全恐慌时，像 Palantir 这种本身表现很好的公司，因为它在某个软件 ETF 里就跟着跌，被拖累。

回到 Reddit 这件事，我之所以纠结，是因为我喜欢个人投资者参与其中，他们感觉能在这个世界里参与，这很棒。但同时我担心他们过度投机，对估值的强调减少了。

长期来看——这就是为什么 Warren Buffett 这么厉害——复利的力量和买入相对合理估值的公司会胜出。 因为你不想在错误的时间持有疯狂的股票，它们会被严重打击。互联网时代有人买了 pets.com 这种东西，从此再也没有恢复过。这很可惜，因为如果他们只是持有 10 到 15 只真正好的，或者买入指数，他们现在会非常富有。

旧卫士 vs 新势力：IGV 软件指数为何下跌 27%？

David： 我们刚才在聊新旧投资时代的对比。如果你看 IGV 软件指数，年初至今下跌大约 27%。可以说市场正在定价这是旧卫士。

Micron 你刚刚说了。 一个理论问题：当现在的创新方向是减少模型中的 RAM 和内存使用，为什么还有人投资 RAM 公司？比如 Google 推出 Turbo Coin 的时候，Micron 股价一天就跌了 12% 或 15%。

John： 我觉得现在大概比那时高了 50%。

David： 它恢复了。但那个消息把它打下来了。

Bonnie： 即使下跌 12%，它当时已经涨了 300% 之类的，记住这一点。

从投资人到运营者：vibe-coding 与杀死 SaaS

John： 首先，仅仅因为某事被宣布，并不意味着它会摧毁整个行业。

让我现在以运营者的身份说话。有趣的是，我的团队大多是工程师，他们非常 forward thinking，喜欢玩 AI 里的新工具，V Code 等等。我们内部——我作为一个聪明的商人而不是程序员——vibe-coded 了我们自己的 CRM 客户关系管理软件。我们摆脱了两个不同的服务提供商，用我们自己的。它是定制的，我们只需要数据库，UI 的东西全是我们自己做的。

然后我们用 AI 做漏洞测试。所以几乎每当我工程团队里有人来跟我说，"哦，看看我们能做这么酷的事"，所有 SaaS 股票就会下跌，安全股票就会下跌。所以如果我还是对冲基金人，每当我的人告诉我一些事，我就会做空这些东西。

但 AI 不能颠覆一切：技术栈的惯性

John： 但他们也会更详细地告诉我一些事情：你不能完全 disintermediate 很多这些东西。很多东西被过度炒作了。

如果你只是 UI/UX、高层的软件自动化工具，那很明显，可持续性会很困难。但你不能 disintermediate 代码——从用户层往下，核心共识、核心区块链、deep tech code，这些还没有被复制。

关于你说的内存问题，你假设了两件事：你假设内存公司本身没有创新、不知道发生了什么；第二，你低估了"业务"本身。这不仅仅是技术，这是他们已经接入的网络、供应链。 当你抽掉一块技术时，并不意味着你可以简单地放进另一块，因为下一块必须与该技术栈中已经存在的一切相吻合。这意味着如果你想要替换，你必须替换整个技术栈。

这就是加密行业正在学到的。 如果你想从零开始构建，肯定有更好的方法。但你在科技世界里有 legacy、有规则、有不能因为尝试全心全意转换为新东西而失败的巨型企业。所以你必须做 step function。

互联网时代的教训

John： 在互联网时代，每次有传言说 Google 要进入旅游业，Expedia、Priceline、Booking.com 的股票就崩盘。25 年后他们还在这里，而且做得更好，Google 不知怎么从未能进入旅游业。

比特币与 Tom Lee 的预测

David： 我想谈几分钟比特币。$76,000 的比特币——如果它收在 $76,000，我们就进入了新一轮牛市。这是 Tom Lee 几个小时前在这个会议上说的，我在读他演讲的回顾。

"You have never in a bear market—if Bitcoin closes up three consecutive months, if Bitcoin closes above $76,000 this month, the bear market is definitely over."

他说 Agentic AI 融资是这轮繁荣会延续的部分原因。这是他的观点。

John： 比特币现在多少？

David： $81,000。

John： 所以他基本上是说已经结束了？

David： 如果它在月底收在当前水平，我们就在 76,000 之上。这是五月。所以我们有 23 天来维持当前水平，熊市就结束了。

John 的观点：旧持有人的换手

John： 我先说：I love Tom Lee. 我认识 Tom Lee 25 年了。我在 Tiger 做 buy side 的时候他作为技术分析师覆盖我，我管理自己的基金时他作为 JP Morgan 的策略师覆盖我。他是一个非常聪明的家伙，知道统计学，能像疯了一样把它们拉出来。所以我绝不想低估他的数据。

我告诉你我怎么想。大概在一月或二月，我觉得我们触底了。我们看到一种 changing of the guard——老一代的比特币人在卖给 Michael Saylor 和 ETF。 这种情况今年早些时候在稳定下来，这是比特币表现更好的部分原因。

我不知道时间——年底、这个数字、那个数字——但我觉得有两件事正在发生，帮助比特币至少在未来几个月走一波小牛市：

  1. 持有人的换手已经完成。 那种不确定性已经过去了。
  2. RIA 渠道获得授权。 这些渠道控制着数万亿美元的财富。当 Morgan Stanley 说"我们将能够用我们的财富渠道做点什么"时，仅仅因为他们被允许，并不意味着他们会立即去做。你需要那些个体 RA 接受教育。 教育完产品之后，他们才推荐给客户。这个过程需要四、五、六个月。现在是五月。所以从年初这些渠道被允许开始向个体投资者推荐，到投资者被教育、终端用户足够了解——我们快到那个点了。

所以仅从这两个原因，加上我认为宏观环境平静且不错，加上 Clarity Act 的希望还在——我们拭目以待——这些都是我认为我们将进入比特币不错阶段的原因。

"Calm Situation" 的真正含义

Bonnie： 你能详细说说"我们处于平静状态"吗？

John： 我说的"calm"是指股东大换手已经发生了。现在拥有它的是新一批人，是一个新群体了。很多大的 OG 鲸鱼要么不再持有，要么已经减持到不需要再减持的程度。

Bonnie： 你觉得为什么会这样？

John： 有几个原因。比特币现在已经接近 18 岁了。我被科技投资吸引的部分原因是我们喜欢创新、新东西，这令人兴奋。我觉得加密正在进入一个新阶段——它不再是 the new new thing。

这就像问"为什么会有连续创业者把公司从 0 到 1，然后交给另一个人从 1 到 10？为什么不从 0 到 10？"因为有些人喜欢 0 到 1，有些人喜欢 1 到 10。所以对那些人来说，是一个自然的退出时点，尤其是流动性终于到位了。

Broadridge × Avalanche：基础设施层的合作

David： Broadridge Financial Solutions（NYSE: BR）——这家全球科技领导者每个月处理 8 万亿美元的代币化资产——正在扩张到数字资产领域，并与 Avalanche 合作。谈谈这次合作的意义。

John： 这非常棒，也非常重要。Broadridge 是传统证券世界的领导者，他们处理 corporate actions、proxy votes，以及很多人没意识到但需要他们来处理的事情。

他们宣布了——我们非常自豪地说——一项交易：他们将在线上把代币化股权放到 Avalanche 区块链上。 很快，Galaxy 的股权将通过 Broadridge 进入 Avalanche 区块链。他们处理所有的 corporate actions，这是传统股权的重要部分。

Avalabs 的整体愿景

John： 这是一个巨大的举动。它如何融入我所看到的世界、Avalanche 和 Ava Labs 的全部意义？

从我们六七年前的第一天起，我们就想既服务 crypto-native 客户，也成为所有机构资产到来的默认基础设施。我们想帮助企业，所做的就是创造这些组件和部分。

我们把它视为两层结构： - 资产层（Asset Layer）： 数十亿稳定币、货币市场和国债、私募信贷、私募股权、基金，甚至代币化的公共股权——所有这些都在 Avalanche 上。 - 基础设施层（Infrastructure Layer）： 这就是 Broadridge 的位置。

每个人都想要代币化的真实世界资产，但他们忘记了：你需要下面的系统来让这些事情顺畅运行。 显然有 crypto-native 基础设施——钱包、法币到加密的 on/off ramp、托管等等。但也有传统金融的东西：谁来做 corporate actions？谁来做幕后所有让你对一家公司的所有权、proxy vote 顺畅运行的东西？合规、监管等等。

Broadridge 就是这样的合作伙伴，是未来代币化真实世界资产基础设施层的一块。

价值捕获问题：谁拿走价值？

David： 对投资者来说，关键问题永远是价值捕获。Straits X 是你合作的新加坡公司，他们在亚洲很大，正在建立自己版本的稳定币。但我的问题是：所有这些在基础设施之上构建的公司和应用——who captures the value?

John： 好，让我们先回到第一部分。Straits X 是很棒的事情。他们有自己的稳定币，明显是面向亚洲的，他们在 Avalanche 上做这件事。这说明了他们对 Ava Labs 和 Avalanche 的看法。

他们也接入了 Alipay、Grab Pay，所以他们有巨大的分发能力。我们很自豪能和他们合作，让他们选择了 Avalanche。

价值捕获的链条

John： 现在你问的是价值在哪里、如何被捕获。这是个很好的问题。

把比特币拿出方程式。Product market fit for digital gold——如果你这么称呼的话。有些人相信，有些人不相信，但现在大致就是这样。它占了 2.7 万亿（或者多少）的 50%。其他所有的，问题是 where's the value capture?

我认为价值捕获必须沿着整条链发生： - 应用层（DApps）： 顶层必须能够运营业务，有它们存在于基础设施之上的理由。 - Layer 1： 也必须想清楚它们的代币经济，才能存活并服务所有这些应用。

Avalanche 的设置是 a network of networks。 你有主链，但你可以 spin up 你自己的定制的、专门用途的、self-sovereign blockchain。很多传统金融人将其放在封闭的私有链中，不是 permissionless 的一部分——他们已经在捕获价值了，仅仅因为他们在自己的私有链上。我们也捕获一点点价值，因为他们付费让我们做这件事。

但最终他们想要一个世界，可以连接到 Avalanche 生态系统中的另外 100 条 Layer 1，最终他们想连接到主链获取流动性。Avalanche 是 EVM 兼容的，所以他们可以连接到以太坊世界和所有那些东西。

所以我认为，开始时客户必须能为自己创造价值——他们已经在这么做了——然后一旦他们开始连接，自然的价值就会流向 Avalanche 生态系统的其他部分。

结语

David： 好的，很好的回答。非常感谢你。祝 Avalanche 的演进和成功继续顺利，下次再聊。

John： Thank you Dave. Pleasure.

David： John, good to see you. Pleasure.

John： Thank you for watching. Don't forget to like subscribe.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽视的悖论：当"无聊"成为最深的护城河
John Wu 顺口说出的"TSMC 是一家 low-vol、historically steady 的公司"——这句听起来像免责声明的话，其实藏着整集节目最反直觉的真相：**在一个把"故事性"作为定价核心的市场里，TSMC 之所以被低估，恰恰因为它太无聊了。**
NVIDIA 卖的是"叙事的斜率"，TSMC 卖的是"工艺的曲率"。前者的估值锚是想象力，后者的估值锚是良率。市场天然给"可被讲述的奇迹"溢价，给"不可被替代的乏味"折价。这就是为什么同样在 AI 食物链上游，NVIDIA 的 PE 像火箭，TSMC 却像在排队领便当——**它太稳，稳到没人愿意为它编故事。**
## 跨领域连接：日本"过劳死"、量化基金的半衰期、与 OG 比特币巨鲸的退场
整段对话里有一个奇异的暗流——John 三次提到"半衰期"这个概念，但用了三种完全不同的语境： - **科技周期**："tech cycles are far shorter than economic cycles" - **量化模型**："these quant models

item.title: [Business of Sport] Premier League Winners & Losers, Enhanced Games Makes it's Entrance, & The World of Rugby League
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个让人意外的系统性观察

一、足球俱乐部的"P&S会计学"正在创造一种隐形的人才走私经济

表面看，纽卡斯尔卖掉Elliot Anderson、切尔西在球员代理费上花掉6500万英镑（接近桑德兰整个球队的工资总额），是各自独立的财务决策。但这两件事其实是同一套规则的镜像产物——盈利与可持续性条款（PSR）把"本土青训球员"变成了一种特殊的会计资产：他们的转会价格是100%纯利润，因为账面没有摊销成本。

这制造了一个荒诞的激励结构：俱乐部不得不卖掉自己最具长期价值的本土年轻人，去换取财务报表上的"利润"，再用这笔钱去买摊销期5年的外国球员。换句话说，财务规则正在系统性地把英国本土足球人才从顶级俱乐部"挤出"，流向中游俱乐部。这是FFP/PSR最隐蔽的副作用——它本意是保护可持续性，实际效果却是让"本土性"成为可以套现的金融工具。这跟美国大学体育的NIL改革产生的"运动员套利"现象高度同构。

二、增强游戏的真正商业模式不是体育，而是一场"去污名化"的制药营销

这是整集播客最被低估的洞见。Aaron D'Souza说"每个中年男人都会问：他用了什么？我怎么搞到？"——这一句话已经泄露了底层逻辑。

把这事放到更广的坐标里看：增强游戏与GLP-1减肥药（Ozempic/Wegovy）的爆发遵循同一条文化曲线。十年前，使用减肥药是禁忌；今天，名人公开使用、Instagram广告铺天盖地。增强游戏在做的事，是把睾酮和HGH推上同一条"医疗化—名人化—日常化"的轨道。Peter Thiel投资它，不是因为他爱看100米短跑——他投的是未来15年"健康跨度"（healthspan）市场，预计规模超过6000亿美元。

更让人意外的关联：这与橄榄球联盟的困境恰好形成完美对照。橄榄球联盟代表"工业时代肉体劳动者的体育"——它的衰落与英国制造业北部的衰落同步。而增强游戏代表"后工业生物科技消费者的体育"——它的兴起与硅谷长寿运动同步。同一个周末的两个故事，其实在讲一场跨越130年的身体经济转型。

三、阿森纳的"赢但赢得没那么漂亮"是一种新型竞争形态的预兆

节目里轻描淡写地提到："阿森纳过去四个月都是在'挤过线'，没有那种3-0、4-0放松踢的酣畅赛季"。这听起来像球迷牢骚，但其实是一个统计学转折点。

跨领域类比：这与国际象棋顶级赛事中Stockfish引擎时代的"+0.3优势"打法惊人相似——当所有人都接近最优解时，比赛不再由天才闪光决定，而由谁能在边际上多积累0.5个百分点决定。曼城在过去七年训练所有英超球队学会了"如何接近最优"，结果反过来吞噬了它自己。

这预示着一个反直觉的未来：英超进入一个"没有王朝"的时代，不是因为竞争太弱，而是因为竞争太均质化。维拉以低于热刺的工资单挤进欧冠资格、桑德兰用全联盟最低开支冲进欧联——这些不是"励志故事"，而是统计回归现象：当所有俱乐部都能买到相似的数据分析、相似的招聘网络、相似的体能科学时，超额回报必然向那些还能保持组织一致性的中型俱乐部转移。这跟过去二十年对冲基金行业里"Alpha衰减"的规律一模一样——当所有人都用相同的量化工具，超额收益反而流向了纪律最严、规模最小、官僚最少的精品基金。

最反常识的预测：未来五年，英超"前六"这个概念会彻底消解，取而代之的是一个"前九或前十"的混乱中产带。这对联赛商业价值是个隐患——叙事变弱了，但对足球本身可能反而是好事。

📝 转录文本

开场：35度的伦敦与赛季末的余温

Charlie Sr.: 我穿着正经的衬衫长裤，试图展现传统英式的"咬紧牙关"精神，在这35度的天气里……而 Charlie Jr. 这边大概可以描述为"沙滩装"。你看不到凉鞋，但相信我，它们就在那儿。

Charlie Jr.: 这就是有张能挡住脚的桌子的好处。但今天是35度，是史上最热的5月天。我穿得像个正常人。而你居然专门为了来录这期节目去买了件新衬衫，就为了显得我不够正式。

Charlie Sr.: 周末过得怎么样？因为这是英超赛季的高潮——自从我们这个播客（在赛季中段、1月份）开播以来，我们就一直在猜测各种"如果……会怎样"的情景，而它们最终都汇聚到了最后一周。

英超冠军揭晓：阿森纳的"必然"与曼城的失落

Charlie Jr.: 上周我们录节目时，英超冠军的归属还没确定。曼城还有一场客场对阵伯恩茅斯的比赛——如果他们赢了，阿森纳就还得在最后一轮拼一场才能夺冠。但很遗憾——或者不那么遗憾——曼城在伯恩茅斯输了，阿森纳成了冠军。

Charlie Sr.: 我猜你已经从你在普特尼的"Stebbings 庄园"出发，前往酋长球场，加入红白海洋一起庆祝了吧？

Charlie Jr.: 我心情糟透了。曼城在第95分钟由哈兰德扳平。我去 Instagram 上看了一圈我那些阿森纳球迷朋友的动态——曼城也加入了我那张"在我最需要他们赢的时候让我极度失望的球队"名单，跟富勒姆、埃弗顿、阿斯顿维拉并列。

Charlie Sr.: 阿斯顿维拉，几乎是单枪匹马地保住了热刺的英超身份——他们交出了我见过最糟糕的表现。

Charlie Jr.: 那真的是我看过的英超球队最差的一场表现。我从没见过一支球队整整90分钟如此漫不经心、毫无章法、毫无头绪。

热刺保级：脸红多于庆祝

Charlie Sr.: 在维拉公园的那场比赛，最终成了热刺保级的关键，两分之差。说实话，几周前我们看赛程时，我可不会把保级的希望挂在那场比赛上。

Charlie Jr.: 西汉姆呢——埃弗顿和曼城3-3，本来希望埃弗顿能放曼城一马、让冠军悬念多一点；还有最后一轮埃弗顿被热刺击败，他们真的让我们失望了。热刺整个赛季在主场只赢过两次。

Charlie Sr.: 那场比赛你看了吗？

Charlie Jr.: 看了。埃弗顿就是看不出有任何要逆转的样子。在赛季最后一天，球场气氛也没有我预期的那么紧张。所以，公平地说，热刺做了他们该做的——41分保级，西汉姆39分降级。这是一个相当高的降级分数线。

西汉姆降级：董事会、球员、还是结构？

Charlie Sr.: 我记得他们上次降级好像是带着41或42分。如果你是西汉姆球迷，你可能会觉得这是一次有点冤的降级。但说实话，他们这个赛季有相当长一段时间表现非常非常糟糕。

Charlie Jr.: 在冲刺阶段，当热刺在过去两个月表现糟糕时，西汉姆本来有机会。但在最后四五场比赛里，他们交出了几场真正的烂仗。比如纽卡斯尔那场——那本是他们的机会。

Charlie Sr.: 接下来，正如我们在深度剖析西汉姆时说过的，将取决于Sullivan和Křetínský之间谁最终成为带领西汉姆前进的那个人。因为从数字上看，球队所需的阵容重塑规模是巨大的——可以想象六七位主力同时离开。

Charlie Jr.: 莱斯特已经走过了这条路——带着高薪、高价签来的球员降级。你能不能从前人身上吸取教训？这将是巨大的考验。看看下赛季的伦敦体育场会是什么样。

Charlie Sr.: 我们上次没提到的一点是：据报道，他们球场的租金会减半——从大约400万降到200万。所以他们的降级实际上让伦敦纳税人买单。

阿森纳夺冠：一个时代的开始还是巅峰？

Charlie Jr.: 看到阿森纳夺冠的庆祝、那些球员上台领奖，这些都是从未拿过冠军的球员、从未夺冠的主教练。它提醒了你，体育中这种时刻有多重要、多特别。

Charlie Sr.: 我两三个月前就说过，从英超的角度看，确实需要一个不同的冠军——不是曼城，也不是利物浦。这个赛季感觉就是那个时机。我会非常自信地预测他们明年还会卫冕吗？不会。

Charlie Jr.: 我担心。我担心这会变成持续十年的统治。曼城在转型期，曼联谁知道，利物浦也不确定，热刺和切尔西连边都还没沾上。

Charlie Sr.: 我倒是觉得瓜迪奥拉发生的事非常不寻常。如果瓜帅留下来，我会强烈看好曼城下赛季夺冠。他的阵容已经平稳过渡到了更年轻的群体。马雷斯卡接手的是一支正在上升的球队和阵容——所以他下赛季需要做多大调整？我觉得不多。

Charlie Sr.: 而阿森纳这边，我能看到一些缺陷：通常那些统治多年的球队都有一条稳定进球的锋线，能3、4、5球轻松干掉对手。但你看阿森纳过去四个月，他们都是在挤过线。如果他们能赢下欧冠决赛——击败这支大巴黎将是真正史诗般的成就。

商业视角看赛季：超额表现与低估者

Charlie Jr.: 我们能不能用商业的视角来总结一下？这个赛季对这些俱乐部到底意味着什么？

Charlie Sr.: 我们可以提一些精彩故事——比如桑德兰，我以前的俱乐部，重返英超第一年就打进了欧战。这是几乎从未有过的成就。我们之前也讲过布莱顿、布伦特福德、伯恩茅斯在低投入下保持前十竞争力。

Charlie Sr.: 几天前我给你发了一张表，把俱乐部的工资单、摊销转会费、经纪人佣金加在一起，对比"按这个总支出预期应该拿到的积分"与"实际积分"。我觉得这张表非常迷人——它揭示了一些真正反直觉的故事。

桑德兰：全联盟最低开支，却拿到欧战门票

Charlie Jr.: 桑德兰，与阿森纳并列表现榜首——比预期多拿了18分。

Charlie Sr.: 工资8500万、摊销2400万、经纪人佣金1100万，总支出1.2亿，是整个联赛最低。然而他们拿到了欧联杯资格。绝对的巨幅超额表现。

阿斯顿维拉：第七支"大六"球队？

Charlie Sr.: 维拉很有意思——其他球队的超额表现都是在没踢欧战的情况下做到的。但维拉不仅在欧战决赛中拿下胜利，同时还在英超拿到欧冠资格。这真的让我开始觉得，如果说曾经有"Big Six"概念，那现在看起来是"Big Seven"了。

Charlie Sr.: 维拉持续保持顶级表现、持续打进欧战，意味着他们的收入在不断攀升。这使他们今年的工资支出超过了热刺。

Charlie Jr.: 工资2.73亿，热刺2.56亿，利物浦4.18亿（英超最高）。维拉能不能再缩小差距，同时保持那种"挑战者"的锐气？利物浦、切尔西现在状态都不在最佳。机会就在那里。

曼城：管理者价值5–10%的实证

Charlie Jr.: 你之前讲过一位主教练的价值大约是5–10%，这张表恰好印证了。曼城这赛季的积分比预期多6分，正好对应一位顶级主帅5–10%的影响。

曼联：被高估的"复苏"

Charlie Sr.: 大众叙事是"曼联在好转"。但实际上，在没有欧战、只需专注英超的赛季里，他们在表现榜上排第几？第11。

Charlie Jr.: 曼联最显眼的数字是1.96亿的摊销——接近2亿。如果你是阿森纳，摊销1.72亿但拿了冠军，那能说得通。但曼联近年的引援众所周知效率低下——比如安东尼的8500万。

Charlie Sr.: 还有些"沉睡资产"——比如从切尔西签来的梅森·芒特，6000万，三年来的贡献极低。乌加特5000万。这些签约就算只是默默拖累，也在拖累整体。

切尔西：行业最大失败案例

Charlie Jr.: 切尔西第二低（-22分），工资3.59亿，摊销2.12亿（英超最高），经纪人佣金6500万（英超最高），总支出英超最高。

Charlie Sr.: 等等——切尔西在球员经纪人身上的支出，几乎接近桑德兰整个球队的工资？

Charlie Jr.: 是的。下赛季没有欧战，对切尔西来说是关键一季。如果说到现在还有借口，下赛季对切尔西来说就真的没有借口了。

纽卡斯尔：周期的尽头？

Charlie Sr.: 纽卡斯尔——排名第14，工资2.43亿、摊销1亿、经纪人2000万、总成本3.63亿，比预期少5分。这是一支踢欧冠的球队，过去四五年一直在上升，但这赛季感觉像后退一步。

Charlie Jr.: 我回头看这几年会想，他们对PSR的管理是不是处理得最好？看 Elliot Anderson 的进步，再想想他们留下了哪些球员——一些花大钱签来的外国大牌。当你手里有一位划时代的英国本土天才 Anderson，本可以围绕他建队，就像阿森纳围绕 Rice 那样。

Charlie Sr.: 还有一个额外因素：作为曾经住在那里的人，看着桑德兰踢欧战会让纽卡斯尔球迷非常痛苦。纽卡斯尔对桑德兰的优越感是难以解释的。

转向英式橄榄球联盟（Rugby League）

Charlie Jr.: 一项新运动——也是请求最多的一项。

Charlie Sr.: Rugby League。这周末是挑战杯决赛。

Charlie Jr.: 我们每周收到多少次让我们聊 Rugby League 的请求？每周都是两位数。还有不少咒骂——"南方的纨绔子弟在评论橄榄球"。但我现在意识到，有时候"posh Southerner"其实是某种亲昵称呼。还有人指出我们每次说 rugby 都没说 rugby union——我道歉。

历史：1895年的分裂

Charlie Jr.: Rugby League 起源于1895年的约克郡，是从橄榄球足球联盟（RFU）分裂出来的，争议焦点是球员是否能获得报酬。

Charlie Sr.: 这个根本问题持续了100年——直到1990年代。Rugby League 130年前就开始以完全职业化的方式运作，而 Rugby Union 直到80年代才开始允许某些形式的支付。

Charlie Jr.: 球员要求的是补偿，因为他们要出差、要离开磨坊和造船厂的工作，损失工资。

阶层与地理：一种身份政治

Charlie Sr.: 这是一项在20世纪上半叶在国家特定地区成长起来的运动，明确局限于英格兰西北部、约克郡和赫尔。这个地带就是 Rugby League 的国度。

Charlie Sr.: 同时，Rugby Union 不仅是业余的，而且是非常"业余的"——他们甚至没有联赛，只打友谊赛和年度淘汰杯赛。

Charlie Sr.: 整个20世纪，特别是战后到70、80年代，你会看到顶尖 Rugby Union 球员转会到 Rugby League，以全职靠橄榄球谋生。对于威尔士球员尤其如此——比如 Jonathan Davies，1989年从 Rugby Union 转到 Rugby League 时是头版新闻。

Charlie Sr.: 当 Rugby Union 在90年代中期正式职业化、并迅速开始支付高薪后，球员开始反向流动。比如 Jason Robinson，从威根的 Rugby League 之星转入 Rugby Union，成为2003年英格兰世界杯冠军队的核心。

规则与体能：最适体能运动？

Charlie Sr.: Rugby Union 是15人制，Rugby League 是13人制；Rugby Union 中争球（scrum）是核心，Rugby League 中它只是重启比赛的方式；规则差异让两项运动给人非常不同的感觉，甚至球员体型也明显不同。

Charlie Sr.: 综合心肺、速度爆发力和纯力量，Rugby League 球员被认为是最全能的运动员。

Charlie Jr.: 我去过威根勇士队（Wigan Warriors）的训练中心，他们刚结束健身房训练——非凡的身体素质。

不只是一项运动：阶级抵抗的延续

Charlie Jr.: 这远不止是一项运动——它是政治表达，是从形成之初就有的反抗。这是一种阶级体系：工业无产阶级 vs Rugby Union 的公学体系。

Charlie Sr.: 这可以用两个词概括：Andy Farrell——他是世界顶级 Rugby Union 教练之一，但生涯绝大部分时间是 Rugby League 传奇人物，也是 Owen Farrell 的父亲。Sean Edwards 也是——Rugby Union 世界顶级防守教练，来自 Rugby League 背景。

商业现实：英国 vs 澳大利亚

Charlie Sr.: 这个周末是挑战杯决赛——相当于英式足总杯决赛。1996年成立的 Super League 让 Rugby League 从冬季项目改成夏季项目，避开足球档期，迎合 Sky Sports 新世界。

Charlie Sr.: 总体来说 Super League 是"成功"的——没有大量俱乐部破产，观众没有大幅下降。但它被澳大利亚联赛远远甩在身后。澳大利亚 NRL 是所有橄榄球联赛中的怪兽，年收入达4.5亿英镑。Super League 年综合收入约1–1.2亿英镑；Prem Rugby 约2亿。

Charlie Sr.: 最近几周，开始有消息说 NRL 可能"接管" Super League。

Charlie Jr.: Sky 的旧合约是每季4000万。24–26赛季合约降到每季2150万，每俱乐部分配124万英镑。对比英超每俱乐部1亿。

Charlie Sr.: Rugby Union 顶级联赛的电视合约约3500万每年，每俱乐部三四百万——Rugby League 直到不久前还有比 Rugby Union 更大的电视合约，现在已经显著更小了。

Charlie Jr.: Wigan Warriors 收入约750–800万英镑，St Helens 850万，Leeds 是最高约1000–1100万。所以收入数字大致相当于英超联赛二（League Two）的顶端俱乐部。

Charlie Sr.: 也就是说，Wigan Warriors 大约和 Grimsby Town 一个收入级别——尽管在全国体育语境下二者完全不在一个量级。

商业挑战：地理、媒体、澳大利亚

Charlie Sr.: 平均每周大约1万球迷出席，Wigan 平均13500——还是顶级。这不足以让这项运动繁荣。

Charlie Sr.: 这次申请加入 Super League 的伦敦勇士队（London Broncos）有大量澳大利亚投资。这种地理鸿沟既是问题——俱乐部都挤在狭小区域；也是商业问题——本地市场没有足够的私营企业营销预算来赞助。

Charlie Sr.: 加上紧缩的媒体版权市场——更少的人付订阅费，Sky 等广播商被迫优先排序，所以他们把 Rugby League 的费用从4000万降到2500万。再加上 NRL 在商业上极其强大，能为顶级球员开出更高薪水。

Charlie Jr.: 澳大利亚最低工资约7万英镑，英国约3.5万。顶级澳洲球员能进入六位数甚至最高接近七位数。Rugby League 在澳大利亚是黄金时段——仅次于 AFL（澳式足球）的最赚钱运动。

出路：拉斯维加斯与 IMG

Charlie Jr.: NRL 已经把比赛带到拉斯维加斯。去年 Super League 也跟进——2025年 Wigan 对 Warrington 在维加斯比赛，福克斯电视台的观众增长了600%，达到37.1万。

Charlie Sr.: 两三年前他们与 IMG 建立了合作关系——使用了经典的企业语言："战略性重新定位运动以最大化其长期商业潜力"。但我感觉是，他们尝试了很多，最终结论是："被澳大利亚接管也许更好。"

民族英雄的缺席

Charlie Sr.: 过去20年里有一件事真正打击了他们：我年轻时，Rugby League 经常上 BBC，英国和英格兰队真的能和澳大利亚队竞争，甚至赢下系列赛。当时像 Ellery Hanley 和 Martin Offiah 这样的人物是全国体育英雄，能进"年度体育人物"候选。

Charlie Sr.: 但今天的普通体育观众可能叫不出一个 Rugby League 球员的名字——因为国家队已经很久不能在与澳大利亚的对抗中保持竞争力。运动要吸引广泛受众，需要竞争、需要对手。

Charlie Jr.: 我们真心希望这一段讨论能引起你们的参与。我猜我们说错了至少八九处。但希望主要的没说错。

增强游戏（Enhanced Games）：体育还是制药？

Charlie Jr.: 从 Rugby League 那个勤奋、扎实、值得尊敬、有历史传承的世界，转向"增强游戏"。

Charlie Sr.: 哦，增强游戏。

Charlie Jr.: 我经常坐在一个化学增强的搭档对面吗？是的，你看不出来吗？

什么是增强游戏

Charlie Jr.: 它是澳大利亚律师 Aaron D'Souza 的脑洞：如果我们创建一个田径竞赛，在合法范围内允许使用兴奋剂和技术增强会怎样？——不是违法，而是当前被管理机构禁止但合法的。如果顶级运动员能服用睾酮和其他物质，他们会打破更多世界纪录吗？人们想看人类在科学技术加持下达到最高表现吗？

Charlie Jr.: 同时——这点很关键——还要建立一个真正给运动员高额报酬的体系，因为他们是非凡的。

维加斯首秀：低于预期

Charlie Sr.: 这次活动一定程度上让人觉得平淡——场地小、规模有限、抱负低。能吸引到的运动员里只有三四位、五六位是从奥运会上认得的名字，大多数选手不算家喻户晓。

Charlie Sr.: 我觉得它的底层假设是愤世嫉俗的。前提就是"反正你们也都知道他们在嗑药"——所以与其遮遮掩掩，不如把它公开。

Charlie Jr.: Aaron 两年前在我们节目上说过类似的话。这次：91%的参赛者承认使用过睾酮，79%承认使用过人体生长激素。

世界纪录与百万奖金

Charlie Sr.: 第二个犬儒之处：他们说我们的运动员会打破很多世界纪录——证明传统体育既不诚实又阻碍人类表现。任何打破世界纪录的人都有100万美元奖金。

Charlie Jr.: 整个比赛只有一项世界纪录被打破——希腊游泳运动员 Kristian Gkolomeev（之前4届奥运会无缘领奖台），50米自由泳20.81秒，打破20.88的纪录。他赢得了25万美元的比赛奖金加100万美元的世界纪录奖金。

Charlie Jr.: 英国游泳运动员 Ben Proud 仅差0.05秒就能打破50米蝶泳世界纪录——也就是离100万美元差0.05秒。

药物并非万能：Icarus 的提醒

Charlie Jr.: 让我想起 Netflix 纪录片《Icarus》——一位高水平自行车手即使大量使用兴奋剂，也只能拿到第八名。这告诉你这些药物只能带你走那么远。要让产品成功，你需要顶级运动员。

Charlie Jr.: 三位头条项目的获胜者——Fred Kerley（男子100米）、Gina Eveleyn（女子100米）和池中的 Hunter Armstrong——都是"干净"参赛的。他们仍想参加奥运会。说白了：他们是为了钱来的。

谁能/不能参赛

Charlie Sr.: 但要明确：当运动员说自己"干净"参赛时，那只是他们自己说的。

Charlie Jr.: 美国反兴奋剂机构周末在现场对声称"干净"的运动员进行了检测。World Aquatics（国际泳联）说：参加增强游戏的任何人——无论是否使用增强——都会被禁止参加他们的赛事。

Charlie Jr.: Ben Proud 和我们另一位女游泳运动员可能不能再两边游了。但田径选手如 Kerley（明年解禁后）可以两边参赛。

系统性原因：奥林匹克体育的薪酬危机

Charlie Jr.: 几周前我们聊过田径——金钱分配给运动员的情况很糟糕，几十年来从 IOC 和世界田联那里榨取巨大价值，但运动员只拿到很小一部分。

Charlie Sr.: IOC 每个周期赚80–90亿，相当于每年20亿，主要是靠田径。我们前几周讨论过网球大满贯，球员要求收入分成25%。如果运动员能拿到 IOC 每年20亿中哪怕一半的25%，那也是巨额奖金——这种诱惑就会大大降低。

Charlie Sr.: 我们能否惊讶于：在 IOC 一毛不分给奥运运动员的环境下，有些人会说"赢一场25万，破纪录100万，传统方式根本赚不到这个钱"？

Charlie Jr.: James Magnussen——成功的澳大利亚游泳运动员，第一个签约的大腕——他在广播节目上谈了两分钟后说自己愿意参加，但要求100万美元世界纪录奖金摆在桌上。说明运动员不是渴望嗑药，他们渴望的是这种巨大的财务机会。

真正的玩家：制药产业

Charlie Jr.: 我的看法——这是大药企的布局。它在通过美丽的体育比赛之眼，对普通公众正常化"性能增强剂"的使用。它是这些药物的营销工具。

Charlie Jr.: Aaron 在2025年发布会上说："每个曾经打过竞技体育、现在腰痛的中年男人都会问：他用了什么？我怎么搞到？"

Charlie Jr.: 看看支持者就明白：Peter Thiel（PayPal、Palantir 创始人，Facebook 首位投资人）、Donald Trump Jr.。这里有大钱。

上市公司：15亿美元市值

Charlie Sr.: 我想看看 Enhanced Games 与大药企在赞助和合作伙伴关系上的财务关联。我没看到任何申报。我之所以提"申报"——因为 Enhanced Games 是一家上市公司。

Charlie Sr.: 当前市值——上市时 EV 12亿美元，首日跳涨20%——所以约15亿美元。公司自己的 SEC 申报中承认（原话）："未经证实的商业模式、有限的运营历史、迄今为止收入微薄。"

Charlie Jr.: 一项远非安全的投资，但这要回到比赛的目的以及注入巨额资金的人的信念：相信这能成为世界最大产业之一——制药业——的巨大价值驱动器。

与 LIV 高尔夫的对比

Charlie Jr.: 这有点像 LIV 高尔夫——有先行者（虽然不是最顶级），有资金充裕的组织（出于不同原因要让它成功），还有钱赚得很少的运动员盘算：他没破纪录是因为他不够好，但如果我服药，我离世界纪录已经很近，可以赚到100万。

Charlie Sr.: 这正是 Ben Proud。如果他们继续办这些活动，他完全可能做到。

频率问题

Charlie Sr.: 一个我之前问你的问题——目前没人有答案：从财务角度让这事对运动员可行，他们不能每四年办一次。Aaron 说过类似的话：我们需要让运动员的职业生涯在四年奥运周期之外更可持续。

Charlie Jr.: 这是唯一允许"增强"运动员参赛的比赛。如果运动员服用增强剂，这将是他们唯一能参赛的赛事，因此需要比赛节奏——既为了赚钱可行，也为了保持竞技状态。

童话的死亡

Charlie Sr.: 想用一个想法结束：LIV 最大的挑战、增强游戏更大的挑战是——童话的死亡。

Charlie Sr.: 那个美丽的童话：年轻人渴望从事一项运动、主要因为热爱去做；因为我们都爱看，他们最终也能赚钱——这是我们都想相信、被教育去相信的美丽童话。这也是为什么足球球迷有时会对球员"为薪水而不是为队徽踢球"非常生气。

Charlie Sr.: 我们谈过美国大师赛是一个美丽的概念——他们留下了桌上的钱。LIV 则相反——它是丑陋的东西，本质上是"我们可以贿赂一批球员加入我们的比赛"。没有美感，是纯粹的经济计算。

Charlie Sr.: 增强游戏是另一个童话被打破的情景。许多人对传统田径、游泳、骑行已经心怀犬儒——能否抓住这批已经犬儒的观众？我有种直觉：失去童话、并对失去童话感到愤怒的人，不太可能转身成为反映他们自己犬儒主义之物的热情拥护者。

一个新的童话

Charlie Jr.: 它创造了一个新的童话。

Charlie Sr.: 但确定的是：这些产品——睾酮补充剂、人体生长激素——是非常高利润的产品，目前在普通人群中有显著的污名。如果在5、10、15、20年里，这些产品能逐渐"去污名化"——人们看到健康的年轻成年人公开使用、表现出色、笑容灿烂——那些市场的增长可能极其可观。

Charlie Jr.: Charlie，顶级话题。下周见。

Charlie Sr.: Indeed.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个让人意外的系统性观察
### 一、足球俱乐部的"P&S会计学"正在创造一种隐形的人才走私经济 表面看，纽卡斯尔卖掉Elliot Anderson、切尔西在球员代理费上花掉6500万英镑（接近桑德兰整个球队的工资总额），是各自独立的财务决策。但这两件事其实是**同一套规则的镜像产物**——盈利与可持续性条款（PSR）把"本土青训球员"变成了一种特殊的会计资产：他们的转会价格是**100%纯利润**，因为账面没有摊销成本。
这制造了一个荒诞的激励结构：俱乐部不得不卖掉自己最具长期价值的本土年轻人，去换取财务报表上的"利润"，再用这笔钱去买摊销期5年的外国球员。**换句话说，财务规则正在系统性地把英国本土足球人才从顶级俱乐部"挤出"，流向中游俱乐部**。这是FFP/PSR最隐蔽的副作用——它本意是保护可持续性，实际效果却是让"本土性"成为可以套现的金融工具。这跟美国大学体育的NIL改革产生的"运动员套利"现象高度同构。
### 二、增强游戏的真正商业模式不是体育，而是一场"去污名化"的制药营销 这是整集播客最被低估的洞见。Aaron D'Souza说"每个中年男人都会问：他用

item.title: [Latent Space] Devin’s 80% Moment: Background Agents, 7x PRs, & End of Hand-Held Coding — Walden Yan & Cole Murray
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：测试不是"计算机使用"，而是"分布式系统推理"

大多数人把 AI 测试理解为"让模型点对按钮"——这是巨大的误读。Walden 在访谈中暗示了一个被严重低估的事实：测试的真正难度不在视觉与坐标，而在于"如何让一个跨前后端的修改在一个被精确编排的环境中复现某个特定行为"。换句话说，测试 AI 实际上在做的是"分布式系统的反向运维"——它必须先理解服务拓扑、再理解 feature flag、再理解权限矩阵、再理解触发路径，最后才轮到点击。

这里有个隐藏的真相：Devon 已经在事实上把"测试"重新定义为"代码库语境下的因果链推理"。这是为什么他们说"没有任何一个 frontier model 能独自完成端到端测试"——他们必须 orchestrate 多个模型协作。而这恰恰违背了 Walden 自己写的"不要构建 multi-agent"那篇著名博客。一个有意思的张力：他在编排层反对 multi-agent，却在认知层默默拥抱了 multi-model 协作。 这是一种"产品层单 agent、能力层多模型"的微妙双重态——一个尚未被命名的新范式。

二、跨领域连接：代码库的"格雷欣法则"——劣币驱逐良币

Cole 提出了一个堪称今年最重要的工程观察："你的代码库会退化到团队中最差工程师的水平"。这不是一个软件工程命题，而是一个经济学中的格雷欣法则在 AI 时代的复刻——劣币（低质量代码模式）会驱逐良币（优质模式），因为 AI 从代码库中学习，并把出现频率最高的模式视为"惯例"。一个 20 层嵌套 if-else 一旦被写入主干，就会成为后续 100 个 PR 的模板。

把这个观察推到极端，你会得到一个反常识结论：未来的 CTO 角色将更接近"生态保护员"而不是"建筑师"——他们的核心工作不是设计新东西，而是主动剪枝、维持"模式多样性"、阻止单一劣质范式的指数级蔓延。Cole 提出的"scheduled cleanup"实际上就是代码库的"森林防火"。

更妙的是这个连接：Git AI 把 prompt 写进 git metadata 的设想，本质上是给代码库添加"DNA 注释"——让未来的 agent 能读懂"为什么"而不仅是"是什么"。这与生物学中"非编码 DNA"承载调控信息的发现惊人地同构。代码库正在从"指令集"演化为"指令集 + 演化历史 + 决策语境"的三层结构。

三、被忽略的根因：infra 决定 agent，而不是模型决定 agent

听众听完整集很容易得出"模型变强了所以背景 agent 可行"的结论。但仔细拆解 Walden 谈到的几个"魔鬼细节"，你会发现一个更黑暗的真相：真正决定 Devon 体验的，是文件系统的网络延迟、VM 快照的增量算法、firecracker 的嵌套虚拟化能力——这些都是 2010 年代云原生时代的遗留问题。

最震撼的一个细节：他们发现 grep 慢的根因是虚拟机的"文件系统"实际上是网络 S3 缓存——每次 grep 都在跨网络调用。这意味着当今所有"AI 编程慢"的抱怨，根因可能不在模型推理，而在 1980 年代设计的 POSIX 文件系统假设在云时代的彻底崩塌。

这里有个让人意外的跨界连接：Devon 的"block diff file storage"本质上是把 git 的差分思想下沉到了文件系统层——他们在用版本控制的逻辑做存储编排。这预示着一个更大的趋势：未来 5 年的关键基础设施创新，不会发生在模型层，而会发生在"为 agent 优化的存储/网络/虚拟化"这个全新的栈上。谁先把"瞬时启动 + 状态持久化 + 增量同步"这三件事在 100ms 内做完，谁就能主导 agent 的实际体验。

而当 Cole 说"我不想为 $20/座位竞争"时，他实际上在说一个深刻的事：agent 的商业护城河不在 agent 本身，而在"你能不能让 agent 跑得起来"的那一层 infra。这就是为什么 Cognition 选择"卖 agent + 卖 agent infra"——他们看穿了一件大多数模仿者还没看穿的事。

📝 转录文本

开场：测试不是计算机使用，而是分布式推理

When people think about the ability of an AI to run your app and test it, I think they actually overindex on the computer use part of it because computer use in my mind is the literal okay you want you know a button you want to click can you emit the right coordinates to go click that button.

I think testing is actually a really interesting problem solving uh challenge for these AIs because if you wanted to do arbitrary testing like imagine you make a change that spans the front end and the back end to actually test that change we have to reason through what how do you first run these applications to orchestrate with each other with the right version of the code then okay how do I trigger the feature or how do I make the thing actually happen. That is where we spend most of our time.

主播口播：感谢订阅

Before we get into today's episode, I just have a small message for listeners. Thank you. We would not be able to bring you the AI engineering, science, and entertainment content that you so clearly want if you didn't choose to also click in and tune into our content.

We've been approached by sponsors on an almost daily basis. But fortunately, enough of you actually subscribe to us to keep all this sustainable without ads, and we want to keep it that way. But I just have one favor to ask all of you. The single most powerful, completely free thing you can do is to click that subscribe button. It's the only thing I'll ever ask of you. And it means absolutely everything to me and my team that works so hard to bring the inspace to you each and every week. If you do it, I promise you we'll never stop working to make the show even better.

Now, let's get into it.

嘉宾介绍

All right, we're in the studio with Walden Yen, co-founder, CPO.

Yeah.

Which is cool title. Um, yes. And one coiner of context engineering.

Yes. Yes. Although I think there were many people who used the terms in various ways beforehand. But um I I I did find that people both internally and externally enjoyed the upgrade from font engineering or you know model wrapping into maybe a more thoughtful way to build agents.

Yeah. For uh for those who haven't caught up on that, I have on screen the don't build multi-agents post which you should read read on and we might refer to. And Cole Murray who created open inspect.

Great to be here.

背景 Agent 的崛起：2025 年的关键拐点

Okay, so let's talk about it. Everyone is building their own Devins. Um, what's going on?

Yeah, so I think the engineering world is kind of waking up to this idea of background agents, cloud agents, uh, whatever you'd like to call it. And I think we saw a shift around the December time frame of 2025 where the models Opus 4.5 and GPT 5.2, to they reached a capability where we moved away from kind of handholding the model and being able to actually more or less autonomously drive the model. And what I mean by that is that we could pretty much go from a specification to a completed pull request assuming the spec was good enough uh with very little friction. And that paradigm alone I think changed a lot of how we interact with agents um and kind of opened this world where background agents became more practical.

I think for for call everyone experienced this in December but I feel like there was just this increasing ramp right like um there was this the moment which was I think sonnet 37 where like you guys rewrote Devon in one night or something.

Yes. Yeah.

Yeah. So describe 2025 or you know how how it felt from your side.

In retrospect you know we always thought it was ramping up but then even now over the last 3 4 months from today we it's been ramping up even faster. So it's almost funny to be talking about how like big of a leap Sonet 3.7 was and we honestly a lot of it was stripping out parts of Devon that were no longer needed with that jump in intelligence. But I also just think that a lot of the recent leaps uh especially you know you look at like models like opus and latest GBT models they are reaching levels of autonomy where people are actually fighting that they actually can't just be hands off and people who were once debating oh you know do I need to be in the weeds with my model in the IDE um can I just completely move it off into the cloud that's a that's a more serious conversation and we've seen that in in all of our growth charts um internally there's this funny graph where our usage has uh of PRs or our merged PRs has grown 7x since I I I forget what

I think Dave uh maybe tweeted that. Yeah. Uh yes.

Um it grew like 7x over like the last I think it was like 2 months, 3 months, something like that. Uh and then you see our engineing headcount growth. It's like gone up by like 10% or something like we were we were afraid to release this.

So, so this is Devon commit percentages on all Devon repos uh was 16% in January and now 80% in March.

Yeah, it's like uh it's a big shift right now. Um and so it makes sense that a lot of people are now thinking about you know buying Devon but also maybe like you know trying to build their own and and there's lots of I I have a lot of fun building Devon so I can see why other people would want to build their own cloud agents as well.

Open Inspect 的诞生

Well, maybe it's it's good to hear like what what initially inspired you to try to build open inspect.

Yeah, open inspect came about uh through primarily my clients observing how they were using tools like cloud web um openai's codeex at the time and seeing some of the friction that they were having with it. Um, primarily the claw web uh was being used through Slack and a big issue they ran into was that these sessions that were launched were specific to whoever called it via Slack and so if a PM was the one who invoked the session and they would then go to pass context engineering engineering can't see the session and that in itself was kind of a dealbreaker because the PM hey engineering can you jump in but there's nothing to jump in on unless they're copy pasting out or you know the single response that came back.

Um and so kind of seeing some of these problems, I had built a similar kind of architecture internally um just to experiment with um kind of test out different ideas as this trend of moving off of local host was starting to kind of become um and as RAMP released their blog post, I had a lot of the pieces for this already in place. Um, and just thought it would be kind of funny to uh see what Claude could do just purely from the blog post and uh on my ex account there's actually kind of a a thread of where I live tweeted like going through this.

Oh wow.

Uh comparing GPT and Claude as both of them are going through it

like on the announcer thing or something else.

Uh right after it got released. Okay. Um we can put it in the show notes.

Um yeah, it was helpful that I had already kind of knew how to verify the system. I knew what I was looking for. I think RAMP did a great job of really illustrating uh the technical aspects of how to build something. Um it was much more than just kind of like hey we built a great system. It was and here's how you can build it too. And so um I resonated a lot with that just with the problems that I was already seeing. And I thought that uh looking around I didn't really see anything in the open source community that um kind of met this type of system. I think there's a lot that run uh in localhost like superset conductor um and many others but nothing that was actually running in the cloud and so um I built it and I thought it was interesting to just open source it and allow anyone to then have a foundation that they can mix and match on top of.

为什么不商业化 Open Inspect

So literally after Devon was launched was there was open devon which became all all hands. Uh I don't know if you tried that or

Yeah. Well, I was going to say one of the things that interested me a lot with open inspect was like you didn't try to go make it then something you you monetize. Um there are a lot of I think these open source projects would then go really try to like raise vis. Um yeah and uh how did you think about that? I I thought that was very interesting.

I thought and kind of just what I had seen across my clients was that having a background agent system is going to become a critical infrastructure within their company. And so because of that, I think that I wanted to open source it so that they could fork it and put in whatever customization they wanted. Um to that question though, I get asked all like, "Oh, are you going to raise are you going to turn this into a service?"

I'm sure you've gotten offers.

Uh but uh primarily I don't want to do that for a few reasons. One, I think that I don't want to compete for like $20 a seat. I think that that is just a really difficult business. I think it's very easy to copy the main pieces of it. I mean, again, like I built this fairly quickly and I think because you are not owning I guess the entire stack, it's hard to monetize. You have money being made at the sandbox layer with Daytona, E2B, many other players. You have money being made at the model layer. Um, and you kind of sit in this weird in between gray area where what are you actually selling? You're selling I guess the infrastructure. You're selling uh the integrations maybe.

Cognition 卖的是什么：Agent + Agent Infra + 企业落地

Um, let's ask the guy what are you what are you selling?

Well, yeah, there's multiple layers to this in in practice. And actually, it's funny you mentioned the infrastructure cuz when we got started building Devon as well, uh we had to go figure out how to make the infrastructure as well because

you had to build this two years before everyone else, you know.

Yeah, exactly. And including like the side, it was not very polished at the start like when we just built it off of raw VMs from cloud providers like EC2, the bootup time was so slow, I think. Uh, and especially the then like turning off the machines, saving them, and then be able to bring them back up again when the when you want Devon to wake up again later. Uh, it would just be out cold for like 10 minutes because that's just how long these systems took. They were not built for this repeated down and up usage. And so we actually had to go do all of that.

Um, and as a result, now one thing we offer when we go and sell Devon to people is, you know, you don't have to worry about all the compute side of things. We'll make it work. will make it work in your cloud if you wanted to. Um, but aside from the product and I I want to go into the agents and and and the tuning of the intelligence part later, but I think a big part of what we do cognition as well is to just make sure that your company learns and uses and adopts these coding agents cuz I think for especially the largest enterprises in the world, you find that there is a lot of people who want to move over to using AI for their day-to-day workloads. Um, but because of the way projects are planned, because um, not everyone is literate in using AI in these ways, uh, having a team of engineers who can actually go in and onboard you, set up all the integrations you need, the automations you need to really get to that level of, you know, leverage with AI is super helpful. And so, total, we do that. We we show up as thought partners to the customers that we work with as well.

架构选择：Harness In-the-Box vs Out-of-the-Box

So let's talk about like architectural stuff. I think that's always u is something that was the topic of conversation between the two of you. Is this uh sort of like the mental model that you want to start with or something else? I I you know I'll just kind of leave the floor open to you guys.

Yeah, I think that maybe we can start here is just kind of a a general what are the pieces of a background agent system and then maybe we can go into some of the nuances of uh decisions that you can make. But I guess like also like what maybe what Walden is saying is is the agent is kind of like in this open code box I guess right like this is infra and then there's there that's the agent and you had this discussion about whether you put the agent in here or in externally can you sort of tease that out

yeah in a background agent systems you have a decision to make of where the agent is actually going to run this is typically described as the harness in the box or out of the box.

Yeah. with running the agent in the box.

Uh you're making some trade-offs by doing that. The negative trade-off you're making is primarily security because the agent is running in that box. Unless you otherwise design it, all of your secrets need to go into that box as well. And given the nature of AI, it can be unpredictable and you could very easily end up accidentally x-filling your secrets um or you know other kind of unintended behavior.

Now the out ofthe-box is the idea that we are going to have the actual agent running not directly in the sandbox and we'll have quote unquote the brain of the agent running in some type of worker uh control plane. That sandbox then is going to serve as the hands where the brain is basically operating and making tool calls into that environment to manipulate it. I guess other trade-off that you're making between the two systems is that um in my opinion running it out of the box is much more complex because uh you have state that has to be managed whereas if you're running it in the box uh all of the state of that agent is actually in the box and yes it's you could persist it elsewhere but it's all kind of localized and you have less concerns to worry about.

I think a lot of that what you mentioned is why we actually from the start build Devon to what we called separate the brain from the machine. Uh the other thing that this allows you to do is reuse any existing infrastructure you have for dev boxes perhaps and so you don't have to worry as much about making a new type of dev box that has all the dependencies the brain needs as you mentioned the secrets the brain needs as well.

Um, like one thing that we've seen some customers run into is like you have a GitHub app and you want Devon, your agent, whatever, be able to interact with GitHub through this application, but then you have different users with different actual permissions. if they are all interacting through the same GitHub app and there's no actual like separation between the system that decides uh what it does and the actual secrets on the machine then you kind of run into an issue where okay it's hard to do the separation but in practice with Devon it's much easier because we just say whatever you put on the machine that is like kind of the scope of basically what the user is free to do what the agent is free to do so only put the most scope secrets on that machine and then the brain is fully not not accessible from the machine. So you don't have to worry about messing with the any of the most secure parts of the brain if the user is free to do whatever they want with the machine.

I was going to just bring I have this like chart from opening uh where I don't know if this is like in the box out of the box that is something that they do use to describe it. Uh and then also recently Enthropic did like manage agents which is this is their thing.

I don't know it's all it's all variations of the same pattern, right?

Yeah. So this would be out of the box.

Yeah.

Which like is is preferable for them because it's less work.

Uh I would say it's more work but it in my opinion it is the better architecture of the two. Okay. It's just uh you're taking on a bit of complexity by doing that.

Repo Setup：永恒的难题

One thing I've not seen a lot of other players do well is how do you manage what's actually on the box? And this can be complex for many reasons. Like let's say you have a big repository that's changing and updating a lot with changing dependencies. How do you make sure that the working environment of the agent actually stays up to date, has all the credentials it needs to, let's say, run the app and test it, all the things you want your autonomous repo set up.

Yeah, exactly. So in internally at cognition uh we call this repo setup. The hardest part of it's been a perennial problem since the start of the company of how do we help people get the set up because not everyone just has you know uh working cloud environments working out of the box and do do you find this to be a common problem with your clients?

Yeah, this is a very common problem and uh through my consulting this is a lot of what I help teams do. Um a lot of teams don't really have great developer environment setups if any. Um, a lot of the times it's go talk to Bob and get the secrets and that obviously doesn't work when the agent needs to actually set this up. And so a lot of that uh most teams are using docker compose or some type of microservices. And so

in prod

not in prod um like with the open inspect you are using this primarily to interact um and make code changes. There is other use cases but um you can hook whether through CLI, MCPS, other tools um you can then hook that into your production systems primarily for like S sur type use cases but you are not uh necessarily like trying to test your prod internal microser through the system.

Docker vs VM：为什么 Devon 选择 full VMs

Yeah, and you mentioned Docker Compose. I think one direction we saw some of our friends take early on was um using Docker containers as a level of abstraction for their models. Um there's lots of reasons I think why Docker containers are not great. Um one thing is like Docker containers not really a true security boundary for one. Uh but the other is like if you are running real applications a lot of times those applications use Docker and then you have to think about Docker and Docker which is like really weird.

Yes.

And so I think part of like the really hard challenge of getting VMs to work, why did we do that? Well, it was because we realized that you actually needed like full VMs to be able to do these types of things. And especially nowadays where there's actually value in running the application and clicking around and sending you screen recordings of these things, like the value just kind of like keeps adding on on top of that.

Um, but it is a decision I I see people run into when they try to build their own systems is oh like do we like in addition to this do we put the agent in the machine or out of the machine? Do we use Docker? Do we use something else? What what do you recommend people nowadays?

I think Docker is a good solution for maybe not running the agent but running your infrastructure because that is more or less the same setup your engineers are probably already using. Um, if they're not, then I don't know what they're using, but uh they're probably already using Docker Compose.

Yeah, I I've always had a small candle for web containers. I don't know if you guys have tried them before. To me, they were like supposed to be like Docker Light.

No, I haven't tried it.

Um, but yeah, I think any environment that you've set up that is a good experience for your developer naturally lends itself to being easy to set up for the agent. And once you figure out kind of that local developer story, um you've kind of more or less solved the agent in a sandbox uh environment setup. Open Inspect does have hooks as well where you can uh run a setup SH script that will pre-install everything. You can then pre-napshot that build so it starts instantly and then there is a a second hook to actually then like restore the state of the sandbox when it comes back. And so you can already have all of those microservices running and basically get the same experience that you would on your machine within the sandbox.

特殊 OS 与 Android 开发

Another thing that we've been thinking a lot about is kind of like different VM service offerings. Have you had customers where they needed like Mac OS specific VMs or like Windows specific VMs?

Not yet.

There are like many technologies in the world that only work on specific types of machines, right? that was a whole other thing the the thing the block diff file storage format which is uh a file system format that we built so that the VMs could be spun up and down very quickly. Basically the the intuition behind this is imagine you have like a terabyte of disk and your agent only like wrote like a 100 lines of code on top of that disk. How long does it say take to like save and rebr up that disc? Um and most systems because you're not optimizing for this case. Uh it's just like on the order of a terabyte of work because you have to like save all of that and bring it back up. In our system we try to build a file system that incrementally builds on top of each other. So every time you save and bring the machine back up, you're you're only doing work that's proportional to effectively the diff in the file system. Yeah.

Well, what are like how are the customers you work with? Do they generally try to then go set up a contract with another one of these third party providers? Do they try to do the VMs in house?

Uh, most of them I see using modal. I think modal has shout out modal.

Shout out modal.

I think modal has a great offering. It kind of captures all of the sandbox pieces you need. Snapshots being a pretty big piece of that. And given that they also offer GPUs, um, I think it's a pretty nice offering as a whole.

Yeah. Uh, no debate there. Um, modal is great especially I think their their container offering is like the most natural and so especially if you are willing to you know forego like the full VM requirements modal is like a really vast place you can kind of spin something up on.

Python vs JavaScript：哪种语言赢了？

Yeah. Is there a point? So models very python and I feel like most workload like has really shifted to JavaScript. I don't know if you guys get the same feeling like so so okay when I started land space and AIE and all these things I was like 50/50 Python and JS right like that's roughly I think that's wrong now I think JS has won I don't know if you guys like maybe I'm overstating it and maybe for cognition know there's like C# and Java and what have you but like for like new green field apps do you feel that do you get that sense does it matter

I think that most of the libraries that I see in the space are Python native first especially in the observability space. Um that said I think that there is a pretty big appeal of having your entire system in one language especially when you have both your front end and back end communicating you can have one central type which is very nice.

Yeah that's my case against modal because then you have to run

I mean you can run JS inside modal it's just like one extra step that like isn't native to the runtime. Um, I don't know if Yeah, I don't use Do you have numbers?

I don't know. The one thing I don't like about Python is whenever AI whenever writes it's Python, it always does like the weirdest patterns that

because it's like mixing two and three or what?

Yeah, I think it's something mixing two and three. Yeah, like the I don't know if you see this. It always tries to do like has attribute on objects. It's like yeah,

but that you shouldn't be doing that. Like it should error if

because it's training on library code. I think it's more of like from what I've seen it's more of like a reward hacking mechanism where it doesn't want to basic yeah it doesn't want the code to fail and so it even when it knows it has the attribute it'll call get at on it um and for a lot of my clients who have moved towards more kind of autonomous coding we've put that in as a lint rule that if you do get after your pull request is going to fail.

AI 代码的可识别痕迹与防御性规则

Oh, this is a fun topic. Can you tell me more like this? Like what else is AI is like a sign of AI uh coding that you have to put guards in.

So we were talking just before this about Opus 4.7. One of the things this new model likes to do is it writes lots of comments. Not like you know it'll like comment on every line, but it'll write like paragraph like PRDS like on top of every function. Um, but I will say to its credit, these aren't slop, you know, descriptions like they were before, okay? Like, oh, here's what this function does. It's like, oh, here's actually the reasoning and why we chose this approach and what the alternatives were and why we shouldn't do those alternatives.

Like, still too much information.

But I wonder if this actually might be directionally correct if you want systems that can self-maintain themselves in the long run. Oh, they write their specs in line context in the code as well. Yeah.

So, you approve

I

but at the same time it's this tricky problem like maybe we'll just give our users like a setting or something uh for like how verbose you want it to be. I I haven't loved it like I just I like the comment but please like get rid of it.

Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

But I could I could see a world where maybe something of the sort becomes reality. I don't know if you guys know about Git AI.

Yes.

Yeah.

We've talked about it. Yeah. Git AI the idea behind it is that if you run an agent the actual prompts you send to the agent should be stored alongside the code inside the git metadata so that future agents can reference it maybe code review bots can reference it um and it's kind of an ideal world where you know your context for why decisions are made constantly lives aside beside your code and so it's like maybe a more hidden version of this like write massive PRDS for every comment sort of approach.

Yeah, I'm waiting for the real bullcase where we just get rid of git altogether. I we're I'm not I'm not there yet, but I'm looking for it because that would be a big shift.

Kind of on the topic of like visible slop, a pattern that I see a lot of cross GPT models specifically is backwards compatibility um at all costs where it's doing these weird import exports so that it doesn't have to modify uh the names of where the modules were. And uh I've seen claude 4.6 six starting to do this as well.

Oh no.

And again, I think it kind of is this like reward hacking behavior where it doesn't want failure to occur. And you can address that through like SEM grap or other tools where that behavior is pretty easy to identify. Um but it's something that you kind of only learn through the trade of just seeing code patterns. Yeah. Um untyped tupils are a really big problem of just like again just throw any in there like dick string any and again you can address those through linting.

本地化测试与 Mock Server

Awesome. Yeah. Any other so like linting any other tools devon review of course uh not so not so free now but you know still use it.

So one thing that I think we try to recommend teams as they use more AI agents it goes back to this like local testing thing. In the end of the day, you want your agent to be able to do the full thing, not just write the code, but actually run it and test it. And a lot of code bases were not necessarily built for this from the start. For example, you probably do want a local DB setup, a local docker compose and and Postgress in order to have it so that you don't need to give your agent any crazy product credentials to actually run and test its code. We've also internally done a big shift to make a lot of our core uh components of code testable as purely local dev without needing to actually like integrate with uh any live services for this reason. And obviously the older the company like the more you have to change to shift in this direction but you know you can use AI to help you perform this migration.

The older the older the company the more you have to change in order to do local dev misunderstanding. So, so you're saying most most people just build with full integration to other stuff and there's no code path to switch it to local

especially in like when there's like lots of different services and you have like microser architecture making that shift the larger the codebase that the harder it is. I guess if you did build it correctly from the very start think possible but also like a lot of companies in the world that got started before Docker was a thing and so yeah you're kind of forced to make a migration at some point.

Well, Devon's good very good at making mock servers.

Yes. Right. So, uh, and know what one of the projects I really wanted, it's like like little snitch. I don't know if you guys have heard of I run little snitch on my computer. There's like a man in the middle, but it it like shows you all the traffic going back and forth. But then from there, you can sort of reconstruct the server, right? And then and then like create local mocks, so you can local mock everything if you just observe traffic for a little bit.

Y, that's an interesting idea.

Um, cool. Uh I I I don't know if this will get anywhere, but I wanted to maybe talk a little bit about the cloud code um uh leak because usually if I have an enthropic person on uh I can't talk about the cloud code leak. Did you guys learn anything from cloud code?

Uh our team was not that uh interested in that leak. We didn't spend that much time on it. But

I'm just I'm just fishing for

uh No, I didn't really uh research too much into it.

Windsurf 2.0：背景与前景 Agent 的融合

Fair enough. Okay, one more last thing before we go. Uh, Windsor 2.0, you guys shipped another thing.

So, the sort of meta context is you use background agents enough sometimes you're going to want to bring them to foreground and like that that little like handoff from local to cloud is hard to work on. Um, and then and Devon has or Cognition has just done it.

Yeah, I I think for me the biggest um gap this is trying to close is again how do you make the testing process as fast as possible? when it can test on its own and send you a video, it's freaking magical. Sometimes there are just really difficult things you can that you do just need to like pull down locally to test. And you know, we just want Windsurf to just kind of be your like local command center of all your agents, like your your background ones, your local ones. And you can imagine, oh, okay, this agent needs me to review something. I'll pull that down, move my other agents to the background, go test it. Okay, boom, done. On to the next one. Right? Um, you have some issue you got to fix in the background, just click like approve. Okay, sort of start a background agent to go fix it. I'd love a world where I'd have to leave this window, you know, then maybe the other window I got to figure out how to stop spending so much time in Slack, but maybe, you know, someday we'll want to get those two as well.

Yeah. And like does that require the binaries to be exactly the same for local versus cloud?

So the funny thing here is that the behavior between local agents and cloud agents, I think that is actually a bit different in their ideal state. I think local agents, you want them to be a bit more fast and let the user make the call on things. Actually, don't try to autonomously go test things. The background agent mode where you go start it off, I think the agent should just assume the next message I send the user should just have everything that the user needs for me and you know not run and stop keep running and don't stop until you have the testing until you have.

So that's that's just a slightly different prompt.

Yes. But for many reasons because of all the work we do to make sure that devon works with different git providers uh that it works with different like oss and VMs like we want as much of that logic to be shared as possible. So for our own practical purposes we try to share as much of it as possible.

Yeah. Yeah. Um I I mean I I can't imagine how much work it is to you know transition back and forth. So congrats on shipping this.

Thank you.

真实用例：SRE、PM 编码、客户支持

Okay. Uh anything else that we should cover before we uh wrap? um just whatever you guys were talking about in your lunch.

Um maybe like use cases like what are like do you find to be like the biggest things that your your clients are trying to do with their cloud agents today?

Do you want to just ask it again so we can get like a clean cut?

Yeah. He was drinking his water. Yeah.

Yeah. The thing I wanted to talk about was use cases. What do you think are the main things that your clients come to you today about? Hey, this is why we want to go set up cloud agents.

Yeah, I think the easiest and most common use case I see across everyone is S sur use cases. Uh the idea that whether we have our alerts in Slack or Data Dog or wherever they're going, we want the agent to be the first responder on that. And that doesn't necessarily mean that the agent is actually resolving the issue, but just being able to collect that context ahead of time is huge because again that agent is integrated into the production logs, the database, it has full visibility and over time playbooks as well for how to address certain issues. And so that's a huge win for teams because instantly you can have a full trajectory of what is going on within the system and oftent times actually a pull request directly from that which is a pretty neat flow to actually experience of like error pull request done. Um open inspect does support a trigger for that as well. So that could happen completely autonomously

from data dog specifically or just

uh it supports sentry. It supports a generic web hook and uh if someone wants to add data dog they can.

Yeah. The other use cases that I see um are for kind of non-builder use cases, whether that's the PM or the marketing team. I'm seeing a lot of uh teams where the idea of who's actually contributing code is starting to change. And in a lot of cases, uh the PM, if there's just a quick bug fix, the PM is not creating an issue anymore. The PM is just prompting through Slack and the pull request is then being created. And so I think that that's a huge win. I I think that that trend will continue where we're seeing uh code modifications happening outside of engineering.

The last common use case that I see is customer support and so where they're experiencing an issue with a customer. They're not entirely sure why this behavior is happening.

Previously that world was, hey, there's a bug when they tried to use this feature. We don't know what's going on. Well, they're now tagging that in Slack. Again, that entire full context is ready. they can then just tag in engineering and have a complete understanding of that issue and completely bypass kind of the the previous pain points of like oh can you get more information from them

the only things I'd add on top of that I think I've seen is like continual security scanning continual security review is a very big one as well the s use case internally we think about it as auto triage because we just want every message that comes in and that's an alert that's a bug report to have Devon just start triaging before anything else. And um we've leaned into this use case so much so that we've basically tried to make it so that you don't ever have to leave Slack to interact with this. So again, making the interactions with Devon super fluid from the moment the report comes in to it responds to report and be able to ask it questions right there with full codebase context about all the issues.

Very related to customer support as well. I think one thing that we found is CLIs can sometimes be like very difficult for people who aren't technical to go and use but uh you know an online chat interface that anyone can go and ask questions and it's super intuitive and doesn't assume you have any technical knowledge but does have access to all parts of your codebase super useful for support for salespeople um anyone who might need to have their questions answered about the codebase.

预算与未来：Hybrid Frontier / Subfrontier

Yeah, great call out. This might potentially be like a very expensive u use case. Is there like a rule sense a rule of thumb on like how much people should spend on this because uh you have unlimited budget but like not other people don't you know like

I don't know if this is an answerable question because obviously it depends on like a lot of factors but like I think it depends really on um how people are using it. I think if people are using it responsibly and they're getting value from it, then you know you can kind of determine the budget. Common numbers that I hear are anywhere from a,000 an engineer up to 5,000 an engineer.

Yeah.

I have not heard anywhere in the realm of like 50,000 an engineer for a frame of reference.

We'll get there.

Yeah. I I've seen I've seen numbers go that high for sure. Yeah.

Um I think that this is also I think going to be a big theme of the coming year is we're going to see very expensive very smart Frontier models and we're also going to see people who say you know what I don't need the Frontier anymore for a lot of the work I do because some Frontier models actually are good enough for a lot of the work. Um also shout out you pioneered smart friend which which is a mix. I'm I'm really interested in a world where you basically have hybrid frontier and subfrontier systems where you use subfrontier part to be really fast, really efficient and call out to the frontier part of the system so that you can still get frontier performance for the most part.

Yeah, I'm trying to search but Twitter search is like completely broken. Like I like the the the from field is just completely gone. It's very sad. Uh because I I really

No worries. Um I I I might have to make a a new post at some point about the the return of Smart Friend.

Yeah. Yeah. I mean, Anthropic is now officially adopted.

Yes.

收尾与招聘

Um Okay, cool. Um I think that's it. Like it's really great discussion. Great having you guys on uh background agents are a thing now and uh everyone's building them. Uh we talked a lot about like the uh the production concerns and like you know why you would want to offer one architecture over the other. Um, yeah, lots lots to look forward to.

Yeah, there's a real zeitgeist in the space right now, I think, for companies to want to drive themselves into these autonomous coding factories and uh yeah, you know, we're doing a lot to try to support that and so you know um any listeners are welcome to to come chat to us about that whether using Devon or you know working with us.

Yeah. Hiring.

Yes, of course.

Uh what what uh specifically you know just like give give like one profile that's like very interesting.

I think people underestimate the the role of like really high taste product engineers

okay

in the space right now and the test is like what have you shipped end to end that is

tasteful product

if you've shipped stuff that you think is tasteful and and you're proud of you know you should you should come talk to us

yeah for me um any businesses that are looking to further their engineering org a lot of the consulting I do is around that teams who are maybe starting their AI journey whether that's with cursor or cloud code. Um, but they're looking for someone to kind of help navigate them through the state-of-the-art and beyond just that initial deployment. Um, as mentioned, there's a lot of lift from you've deployed the background agent to how do we actually get this fully integrated into the company and really realizing the true value of that.

Yeah. Okay. Well, thanks you guys for coming on.

Cool. Thanks for having us.

Yeah. Thank you.
