item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Loeb 的"进化"不是策略迭代，而是身份重构
大多数投资人谈"进化"，指的是工具箱扩容——多加一个因子、多看一个行业。但读完 Loeb 的轨迹，你会发现他做的根本不是"加法"，而是**反复杀死那个让他成功的自己**。
- 1995–2013：他靠 Greenblatt 的事件驱动框架赚钱，明确说"我几乎不考虑商业质量、护城河、ROIC"。 - 2013 之后：他承认那套打法养出的同行"没能活过来"，于是把整个团队从 generalist 改组为行业专家，把核心信仰换成 *Quality Investing*。 - 今天：他甚至说"今天你必须是 tech person"——这是一个从 distressed debt 出身、把 Greenblatt 奉为圣经的人说的话。
**真正惊人的不是他的转型本身，而是节奏**：他几乎是在每一个"上一代赢家集体阵亡"的临界点前完成切换。这意味着 Loeb 的 alpha 来源不是任何一种风格，而是他对"风格何时会被时代抛弃"的元判断。换句话说，他的核心能力不是选股，是**对自己当前打法的剩余寿命有定价能力**——这是绝

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] Rebuilding Global Banking With Stablecoins and AI | Deep Dives with Jeeves CEO
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：「困难即护城河」其实是一种反向时间套利

Dileep 说"difficulty is defensible"，听起来像创业者的自我安慰，但仔细看 Jeeves 的数据会发现一个隐藏的非对称：他们用 3 年时间建的「难做的基础设施」（自有 ledger、自有 BIN、25 国本地牌照），在 Stablecoin 时代变成了 3 个月就能撬动新市场的杠杆。

这是真正的反直觉之处——Jeeves 看似在 2021–2023 那段时间"走慢路"，实则在偷偷积累一种「可重用的底层抽象」。当 Genius Act 通过、稳定币合法化的那一刻，他们的边际扩张成本从 70 万美元/8 个月骤降到「3 个销售人头」就能开一个秘鲁。

这其实是 AWS 的故事在金融业的重演：亚马逊先为自己建了笨重的基础设施，然后把它变成了对外的商品。Jeeves 的 CMS（中间抽象层）就是它的 AWS——只是没人这么看它。

更出人意料的是：他们之所以能 4 个人做下 30 亿 TPV 的承销，根源不在 AI 模型有多强，而在于他们 3 年前坚持自建 ledger 留下的数据结构是 AI-ready 的。AI 红利不是均等地降临到每个公司头上，它降临到那些"数据本来就干净、结构本来就统一"的公司头上。

二、跨领域连接：Jeeves 正在做的事，本质是"集装箱革命"在金融业的重演

1956 年 Malcolm McLean 发明集装箱时，所有人都觉得这只是个铁盒子。但集装箱真正改变的不是装货方式，而是让港口、卡车、轮船、仓库都被迫围绕一个统一标准重组，最终把全球贸易成本砍掉 90%。

Stablecoin 之于 Jeeves，就是金融业的集装箱： - 以前：墨西哥 peso → 美国 USD 要经过两个 correspondent bank，像 1950 年代的散货船一个个倒货 - 现在：peso 进来立刻打包成 USDC（标准集装箱），任何"港口"（国家、卡组织、银行）都能直接接驳

但真正出乎意料的洞察在这里：Dileep 提到他们卖的时候不提 stablecoin，而是叫"Jeeves Instant Pay"。这正是 McLean 当年的智慧——他从不跟客户讲"集装箱原理"，他卖的是"门到门 3 天送达"。

任何颠覆性基础设施在商业化的第一阶段，都必须把自己伪装成一个产品功能。 等到客户离不开了，再揭示它的本质。这是 Jeeves 与那些"教育市场使用 Web3"的失败者最深的分野。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：Jeeves 的组织结构正在向加密协议趋同

注意这几个看似无关的细节串起来： - 员工从 200 减到 140，营收增 10 倍 - 4 个人做 30 亿 TPV 的承销 - 边际开国成本从 70 万降到 3 个销售人头 - 一个国家的 GM 是「全权本地代理」 - CEO 自己每周开 3 个 stablecoin standup

意外的发现：Jeeves 的组织形态正在从"传统跨国公司"演变成一种类协议结构（protocol-like organization）——CEO 是协议升级提案者，AI 是执行层，本地 GM 是节点验证者，stablecoin 是共识层。

这解释了为什么 Dileep 一再强调"你不能外包 AI 给 Chief AI Officer"。在协议型组织里，节奏（cadence）本身就是核心代码。一个 10x 的 AI 原生公司之所以能碾压 3x 的"传统加 AI"公司，不是因为他们工具更好，而是因为他们的组织时钟频率就是不同的物种。

一个赠予你的额外预言

Dileep 提到的"把应收账款 token 化成链上抵押品换取 60–70% USDC"——这看似只是个融资创新。但如果你把视野拉远：当全球中型企业的现金流都变成可编程的链上对象时，"国家信用"作为商业银行的核心定价权将第一次出现替代品。

Jeeves 不只是在建一家银行，它在无意中成为主权货币让渡商业话语权的早期催化剂之一。这一点连 Dileep 自己在访谈里都没明说，但他说的"money is going to become programmable"已经埋下了这颗种子。

下一个十年最大的金融变革不会发生在华尔街，会发生在阿根廷、巴西、墨西哥这些"被迫提前进入 stablecoin 实战"的市场。这才是 Jeeves 真正押注的东西。

📝 转录文本

Rebuilding Global Banking With Stablecoins and AI | Deep Dives with Jeeves CEO

开场：核心愿景与冲击性数据

The goal with Jeeves is really to build a global business bank that can function in multiple countries.

Stablecoins is a lived experience. It's not something that's a theory. Like in Argentina, 60% of the population use stablecoins. Our revenue has grown 10x. Our volume has grown 8x. This just wouldn't be possible without it. Money is going to become programmable, and hopefully we're at that, you know, nexus of all of this happening.

Often when companies start in financial services, just because there's so much product to build, the tendency is to start with the smaller guys, grow with them, and then eventually earn the credibility to get with the bigger guys. You took the opposite approach. How did you become relevant to relatively large companies fairly quickly in the journey?

Our underwriting team today is four people. That just wouldn't have been possible 2 and 1/2 years ago. You need 15 people just to get that off the ground. That's what's changed. If you are not AI pilled, you're not going to make it.

一、个人背景：从尼日利亚到 Jeeves

Dileep, welcome to the podcast.

Thanks for having me. I'm super excited.

Before we dive into Jeeves, I don't think a lot of listeners know your backstory, which is pretty interesting. You grew up internationally, you've got a technical background, you started another company, sold it for over $100 million before this. Maybe tell us about you and how that led you to starting Jeeves.

Yeah. So I was actually born in Nigeria. I grew up in three or four different continents actually. So we moved from Nigeria to Doha, Qatar. That's where I spent most of my childhood. And this was when Doha was like a one-camel town — like they didn't… I mean, it's funny looking at the airport now which is massive. It used to be an airport like two stalls and plane A or plane B, and you get on one of them and that was the airport. So it's very small.

And then we moved to Florida, outside of Orlando, probably about 25 years ago. What I've kind of noticed is, you know, the company that I'm working on now, Jeeves, is also a global company. So it's almost like a manifestation of my childhood and the fact that I grew up in all of these different places.

I then went to school for engineering. So I have actually two engineering degrees, which now I feel a lot more unleashed with all of the stuff with AI because I can start building. But the funny story is my master's thesis was actually on using genetic algorithms to mimic money markets. And that was basically LLMs — which is basically you'd have an end goal and it would come up with test cases, rank them, and it's just wild. This was in 2006, and it's amazing seeing that full circle 20 years later with everything that's happening with AI.

After that, I started a company that was in the marketing automation space. Right before that, I was at Stanford GSB. That company was PowerInbox, which we sold for about 106 million, and then I started Jeeves. So, you know, basically at this point, if I don't start a company, I'm unemployable.

But yeah, it's been a great experience learning a lot of different things. I think what I love about Jeeves most is it's just such a steep learning curve. Like one day you're doing banking in Brazil, next day you have to be an expert on stablecoin. And if you don't like the learning curve, it can be so exhausting, but if you like it, it's very exciting. That's the biggest thing that I find interesting about the day-to-day.

二、Jeeves 是什么：产品现状

Amazing. So we're going to talk through the backstory, but just to ground us — we're you're 5 years into the company. What is Jeeves today?

So Jeeves is a stablecoin-native financial operating system for global enterprises. We have two core products:

  • First: Expense management. We sell corporate cards that we offer to our companies, and we can do that in 25 countries. Our core markets are Brazil, Colombia, Mexico.
  • Second: Payments. We do full local payments and international payments. Today our international payments — more than 50–60% — settles directly on stablecoin. And that's been powering a lot of the growth that we've seen in the last year.

三、护城河之一：基础设施

One of the most interesting ways to understand Jeeves is through your moats. Now they're moats — I would say they were massive challenges to building the company and getting scale, and now we can classify them in the realm of defensibility. So two big ones: infrastructure and regulation. Start with infrastructure, which is my personal favorite because it looks like unsexy plumbing, but then the decisions there are really what drive the products you're able to offer, specifically the differentiation. And I think where you started in Latin America, an added challenge is, unlike the US, there's no "as a service" for all of these different pieces. So maybe talk us through — what did you splice together, what did you build, and how does that enable you to drive different things?

It's a really good point. Internally we have this saying: difficulty is very defensible — because it's difficult, it's actually defensible. And we've done a lot of difficult things from day one.

Just to give you an example: our core IP — what we consider our core product, so to speak — is not the front-facing product. It's the layer in between. We connect to 50–60 different partners, different vendors, and we abstract all of that information into the central layer. We call it a CMS.

Just to give you an idea of what it can do: - We maintain our own ledger in 25 countries, and that is really hard to do because you have to do that across currencies, across FX. - We issue statements in 25 countries in less than 24 hours. That took us 18 months to figure out. And today we can do it — and that's a core skill set, because then the ledger sits directly with us.

We sit directly on the card stream. A big difference where we play on the card side is we're full principal members, for instance with Mastercard. So when we issue the card, it says "issued by Jeeves" — Jeeves Brazil — there's no bank in between. That obviously requires a certain level of sophistication to be able to run, and we've learned that over time of how to build that. But we try to go very, very close to the infrastructure stack in all of these countries that we play in.

卡发行的四层结构

I'll give one more example on the card side, because I think this might open up how we think about it. When I think about card issuing, I see it as four components:

  1. Top of the funnel — customer acquisition
  2. The processor
  3. The program manager for regulation and compliance
  4. The issuing BIN from Visa or Mastercard that gives you that license to issue the 16 digits

What we do — the first three — sits on our own stack. Even though we have different partners, if we were going from Mexico to Brazil, we only have to change the BIN. If you tomorrow wanted to launch Indonesia, we only have to change the BIN. And now with stablecoin, even the BIN doesn't have to change, because you get one BIN and you carve it up by different countries.

So it's a super exciting time to be building, and part of the reason we're seeing this acceleration on stablecoin is because we did the hard work of building our own infrastructure stack for 3 years.

为什么本地 BIN 重要

Yeah. So for people who are maybe less in the weeds of infrastructure — why would it matter to someone like Nubank that you have a Brazilian BIN versus, you know, a US BIN?

The value is that the customer experience is very seamless. One of the things at Jeeves is: if you log in to Jeeves in Brazil, Mexico, Colombia, it looks the same. You're used to seeing the same cards in the same places. And you can't do that unless you sit directly on the rail.

When we started — which I think you might remember — we actually were shipping US cards.

Remember? Yes.

We were shipping US cards because we didn't have the setup yet. It takes time to get the licenses. It takes time to get the processors. All of those things. Today, in every country we operate in, we have local issuing and local licenses. What that gives us is the experience for companies like a Hotmart, for a Burger King — it's seamless. You don't have to train the team three times for three different logins for three different countries. It looks exactly the same. You can't do that unless you sit on the rail.

Well, and your acceptance is probably higher, you're able to onboard sort of…

Yeah. Our acceptance is super high because we are sitting directly with our own BIN. In Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, we have our own BIN that's just for Jeeves. The second part of that is we can also build in much better card controls — because again, we do the actual authentication when you swipe it, we say yes or no. We also do some of the fraud detection components, obviously with partners like Sardine. But all of that sits directly with us.

四、护城河之二：监管牌照

Okay, infrastructure. The next critical but often tedious topic is regulation. Maybe talk us through — we've been at this for sort of five years at this point. I think you've got a lot of your licenses, you've got another roadmap. What have you built there?

Where we sit, we sell directly to mid-market and enterprise companies. So we do not really sell to the long tail. The reason I bring that up is it's really hard for us to gain the trust of these companies without the right licenses.

  • We are a regulated entity in Mexico. We've filed for our money movement license in Mexico as well.
  • In Brazil, we're actually filing to be a full deposit-taking bank. So it's a very high bar for a license.

If we don't have this license, again, we don't have the full customer flow. We can provide some version of the cards, we can provide some version of the payments. But to really be kind of the global business bank, enterprise bank, financial operating system — you need to own accounts as well. And to own accounts, you need these licenses.

So we have always looked at licenses as a core defensible moat — as in, the product we want to build cannot be built without licenses. The issue with licenses is it takes time. So part of the way I think about building is: how do you get into market quickly, get a lot of market share, and then you just have a team that just every day is building layers and layers of this license. That way the customer experience hopefully is seamless — they see the same thing, but on the back end, the product is a lot better because now the licenses sit directly with us.

牌照与利润率

The second part that is really important with licenses and with infrastructure is it helps us to expand the margin. Our margin has expanded from 40% two years ago to north of 80% right now. You cannot do that unless you own the infrastructure and unless you have the regulatory coverage — because then you get the whole lion's share, and then you pay it out to partners.

So that's the positive side of having all these things. But it's really hard, and you need a team that — that's what they're doing. We go country by country, and even now, I would say we probably need to get four or five more licenses in the regions we're in to get to the stage that we want to get to.

五、为什么直接切入大企业

You mentioned you're a little bit more enterprise versus smaller company, where I think often when companies start in financial services, just because there's so much product to build, the tendency is to start with the smaller guys, grow with them, and then eventually earn the credibility to go with the bigger guys. You took the opposite approach. How did you become relevant to relatively large companies fairly quickly in the journey?

It's funny because we started with a little bit of doing everything for everybody. And then, you know, 2023 was kind of the year that forced focus.

We were realizing that we were just not making that much money in terms of revenue from smaller businesses, and we needed the same support for them. We're a very lean company. We have less people today than we did two years ago, and our revenue has grown about 10x.

What I'm trying to get to is: we're not built to sustain 10,000 small businesses. That's just not our model. What we're built for is these real mid-market enterprise companies that we sell one product to, and then we cross-sell six others, and we increase the retention, we increase the gross profit that we have with these companies.

It was a little bit of: we did everything, and then we made this really hard decision in '23 to say, okay, which is the audience that really resonates with the product, and what's the product we're looking to build? At that point we did have to make the hard call to debank some of the smaller businesses that we had. But I don't think we'd be here today if we didn't make that hard call in 2023.

从卡到支付的产品演进

That started putting the focus on what we are offering. Before '23, we did not have a payments product. When you go sell this to enterprise, payments is a huge, huge part of their business. In fact, card volume is actually much smaller than payments volume — because if you look at share of wallet, AP (accounts payable) is a much bigger box than corporate card.

We started with cards, we started with these versions of credit products. We turned all of that off. We were like, what's our focus? It's enterprise, it's mid-markets. What do they need? They need accounts, they need payments.

Then how do we make sure we only sell into that subset? We have requirements where, if you're below a certain revenue size — and that goes from somewhere between 10 million ARR all the way up to 100 million ARR — those are the two segments we have. We just wouldn't onboard you. We can't onboard you.

That was a hard decision because it also then focuses on companies like Y Combinator, where we came from, and they've been incredibly supportive. We've had to find ways of like, okay, can we squeeze that in, because those are the companies you want to build in, but they tend to be smaller to start.

Yeah, so maybe a special YC exception here.

六、产品打包：从卡到全栈

Customer point — maybe just a good way to understand Jeeves's product suite is to take a company like Nubank, which is Brazil, Mexico, Colombia. You landed with cards. Do you want to just walk through — you land with this and then where does it go?

What's interesting about our product suite is we are global, but we are fully localized. It took us 2 years to figure out how to sell in Brazil — and a big reason we were not selling in Brazil — and Brazil is actually going to be our biggest market this quarter. It really started turning in September of '24, and the reason was: we started localizing the product.

We made it obviously in Portuguese, but it's more than that — it's tying it into the actual way people use the product. Brazil has an incredibly sophisticated financial ecosystem. I would say in some sense even more sophisticated than the US. There's no cash. Everything is digitized. It's instant. In fact, when they come to the US and ACH takes two days, it's almost like a year for sale.

Exactly.

It took us a while to figure out how to localize the product. The reason I think that's important to your question of "how do these companies use us": we usually start with the card and then we sell second and third products.

  • In Mexico and Colombia, that usually moves into Jeeves Pay, which is our payment product.
  • In Brazil, we actually have a very strong travel product.

If you think about Brazil, a big concern there is fraud. It has one of the highest fraud rates in the world. We had customers that would come to us and say, "Hey, if you have cards that are automatically turning themselves off — meaning they're single use — we would use that for all transactions." So we started playing around with: okay, let's build out a cards API that we now provide to these different companies.

When you think about Nubank: you have corporate spend, and then you have travel. Those are the two areas that we work with them on. That travel component is strong in Brazil but might not be as strong in other countries.

So we have this strong primary product, which is usually the card, and then the secondary product changes depending on the region that we're in, and then that tends to be localized.

七、Why Now：稳定币与 AI 的双引擎

We were fortunate enough to lead your Series A. And every investment has a "why now." The "why now" at Jeeves when you started — which I felt pretty strongly — is I had a broad portfolio in Latin America, all who had tens of millions of dollars in their bank accounts and they could not get corporate cards. And if they could, they'd be from incumbents that made US incumbents look like the most tech-forward companies on the planet. So that was a good "why now." I think now you've got two more which are even stronger. AI and stablecoins. And so before we maybe dive into specifically for Jeeves, as you think about the region of LatAm and the countries that you're in, those tailwinds I would argue are even stronger there than in the US.

稳定币：拉美的真实生活方式

Stablecoin — let's start with that one because in Latin America, stablecoins is a lived experience. It's not something that's a theory. Like in Argentina, 60% of the population use stablecoins. That's not something you have to train people how to use.

In the US, it's a little more like, "Okay, why would I use stablecoins? Because I don't quite get it. Because I have a stable US currency. Amazing. That's fantastic." A lot of countries don't have that. So the fact that you have this dollar-denominated coin that you can almost instantly transfer your local currency to is a huge selling point.

What we started realizing — and a big part of this was the Genius Act passing, which started making it more kosher to talk through stablecoin — was that a lot of the infrastructure we had could now be adapted towards stablecoin. Going back to your question earlier: the reason we can do that is because, again, we maintain our own ledger. So we don't have to have two separate ledgers — one for USDC, one for USD. It all feeds into the same thing.

As soon as the Genius Act passed, it was one of those things where I would say it was a little bit founder-mode. I was just like, "This is happening. Like, this is not debatable. We're going to go build this whole thing." In fact, even today — I mean, you can ask our team — we have three standups every week, and I run them directly. Because to me, it's that important that we move everything onto stablecoin. I see that as absolutely the future. And specifically for LatAm, which is one of the highest corridors of stablecoin usage, it just becomes something that people know how to use, and the product becomes a lot better.

卖产品而非卖技术

The second part on stablecoin that I think is very interesting is: when we sell it to these enterprises, we actually don't pitch stablecoin directly. We pitch it as "Jeeves Instant Pay." But the inside is stablecoin.

The reason that's important is: when you talk to a CFO at an enterprise company, they don't care as much on the technology side. They care: can I trust you? Is the money going to show up? Is this something where the cost is better than what I have right now? If they don't think the money is going to show up, it doesn't matter what the technology is — because at the end of the day, they're going to get probably fired.

So we have to win that trust. We almost kind of guarantee it with the Jeeves brand of like, "Hey, you use us for all these other things. Trust us on this, and we'll make sure it shows up." That's how we sell the product in region. That's been a huge driver of the volume we're seeing. Our TPV has increased from about 400 million two years ago to north of 3 billion right now. And we're probably going to touch about 6 billion this year. So it's significantly increased. A big part of that is because we have the stablecoin as the infrastructure in between.

法币轨道 vs 稳定币轨道

So maybe contrast the — and you've got some more innovative products coming out — but to start with the sort of classic cross-border: contrast fiat rails versus stablecoin rails, because you were serving both customers.

If you think about where we were maybe 18 months ago, before we had stablecoin, and you wanted to do a payment between Mexico and the US, you would usually have to have two different correspondent banks in between. You'd send the payment in Mexico, we'd have to make sure that we had a bank partner that could handle it in the US, because in the US you need to know the source of the funds as well. So we basically had two different bank partners that had to do a handoff to transfer, let's say, a 1,000 pesos and get it to show up for $50 in the US.

Now what happens is: in Mexico you can send us a 1,000 pesos. We collect it on the ground in Mexico. As soon as we collect it, we release USDC, and that settles instantly. And then we pay out on the US side. The longest leg is actually the US payout — because in Mexico that settles instantly, and the ACH still takes one day, but it's much, much, much faster.

For the end user, we don't sell the stablecoin. We sell accounts payable. We sell: your invoices are reconciled. We sell: put in your tax ID, we'll download your invoices and send international payments. That's what we sell. It just so happens that the infrastructure that it runs on is now a lot better, because we can do that in one hour instead of one day or two days.

We give the end user the choice. If they want to use our SWIFT rails and get the MT103 kind of confirmation, we can do that for you — it will just take longer, but that's your choice. If you want to use a faster version, which we call Jeeves Instant Pay, you can do that in the product. You just have to click a dropdown and select that. So we give you that choice — but a lot of the way we sell it is, it's part of the product.

Yeah — would you like your money in a day or five days?

You'd be surprised how many take one day.

Not surprised. Yeah, exactly. Not surprised at all.

阿根廷：稳定币卡的首发地

Amazing. And then coming back to Argentina, which I think anyone who's been there, lived there, can get a sense of — your money at the end of the year is worth nothing close to what it was at the start of the year. You're about to launch a stablecoin card. Who is that for and how is that going to work?

Taking a step back — and you probably remember this when we first chatted — the goal with Jeeves is really to build an enterprise version of Revolut. A global business bank that can function in multiple countries. To be able to do that, you have to launch multiple countries.

Previously, launching a country is a big undertaking, because you have to first get local entities. You need to get local issuing licenses. You need to be able to collect funds. So it would usually take about 8 months, and that's very fast. We were probably the fastest to be able to do that in 8 months, and that would cost maybe 500–700K to just get spun up.

Now with stablecoin — and Argentina is our first new country launch in 3 years, we're super excited about this — you can do that whole thing much, much faster and much cheaper.

In Argentina, we have our own pay-in rails. So you can pay in on Argentine peso. As soon as it comes in, it converts to USDC. So you instantly get currency volatility protection, which in Argentina, where currency devalues, is a huge, huge selling point.

As soon as it converts to USDC, we can issue corporate cards on it. And now if you think about our core product, it's not necessarily just the stablecoin or the payments. We sell the layer on top. We sell the operating system. So now we can sell budgets. We can sell approval flows. We can sell teams. We can sell card controls. We can sell fraud controls. All of that can now work.

What's really cool about this setup and very unique compared to a lot of other stablecoin cards in the market is: if you swipe that card in Argentina, it does not have an FX fee. The reason that's important is the customers we go after are mid-market and enterprise customers. They have 50 employees. You're not going to get a card for 50 employees if you're going to get charged 2% when they buy a Coke. That's just not going to work.

So it's a really cool version of a product that we've used a lot of different technologies to piece together, but now allows us to sell the full product suite in Argentina.

阿根廷作为蓝图

Tying this back to what Argentina is going to become: it's going to become the blueprint for other countries.

Take Peru, for instance. Nobody's going to launch an expense management suite for Peru. It's too small of a TAM. Now we can. I basically have to staff two salespeople in Peru. They'll sell this product out there. I can onboard 10 companies and it can still be a profitable market.

The cost is completely collapsed in a way that previously no one's going to launch in Peru. Now we can launch in Peru because my cost is three sales headcounts. It's amazing.

八、AI：另一个引擎

So I want to shift gears a little bit to AI, which I think has been the other "oh thank god." Like for instance, I think one of the things we all underestimated was just KYB in, for instance, Mexico — and just the volume of documents you need to collect, what you need to link to languages.

Exactly. So that is an example. But maybe talk through — where are you using it in Jeeves, and then what is the impact that it's had on the business?

It's one of those things where, just like a lot of companies, we've tried to figure out how to incorporate it across the board. I would say some functions are seeing a lot more adoption than other functions.

承销：4 人做 30 亿 TPV

If you think about anything tied to document ingesting, we've seen a lot of success there. So I'll give you an example. Our core product is like a 30-day charge card — like a Ramp or Brex. That still requires us to cover the customer spend for 30 days. So it's not a credit card. You can't keep it open. You have to pay us on day 31. But for 30 days, we are covering your spend. That requires underwriting.

Our underwriting team today is four people, and that team is doing 2–3 billion in TPV. That just wouldn't have been possible 2 and a half years ago. You need 15 people just to get that off the ground. That's what's changed. The models are better. It's self-learning. One person that's really smart can make this work in multiple different regions.

That's one example, but we're seeing that across the board. The only real headcounts we're adding are some components of engineering and product, and then go-to-market. Go-to-market is one area where we haven't had as much success using AI — we're trying different components for enriching data, etc. So we do hire humans there. But on almost all the other functions, we're seeing a significant uplift using AI.

The biggest number is: our headcount today is 140 people, and two years ago it was 200 people. Our revenue has significantly increased, and that's why you see that margin expansion. Basically revenue is going up and costs are the same or going down.

客户服务与本地化细节

Well, and customer service, I think, is another notoriously challenging in multiple different languages, multiple different countries.

Customer service is one where we do have our own internal kind of chatbot. We call it Lenora. It handles most of what people bring up.

I think what's interesting — and it's taken us a little while to learn this — is just the cultural nuances. Everybody's got OCR, but it took us a while to figure out that in Brazil they use the commas and the dots interchangeably. So when you're doing underwriting and you see the commas, the commas are actually the dots, and vice versa. That training is what needs specialization, and that's again specialized to just Jeeves and what we do.

So yeah, it is something where we've seen it across the board: customer service for sure, KYB, document ingestion is another area that we've been using that as well.

九、未来：链上化与可编程货币

Great. And so now I want to fast forward a little bit to the future. You gave us a little bit of a preview about this, right? It's getting easier to launch more countries, things are coming more global. As you think of a global business bank two years from now, five years from now — what does that look like? What might be a little less intuitive to anybody?

I actually think a lot more is going to go on-chain. There are two or three things that we're thinking about.

1. 统一资金池

If you think about a business like ours, we have to still maintain capital pools in different markets. We have to maintain pesos, BRL, Mexican pesos, Colombian pesos, etc. We maintain a lot.

What would be very interesting — which is what we're starting to dabble into — is actually just keeping a single source of USDC or USDT and minting when we need it. That way the cost of capital significantly drops, and we don't have to maintain these pools.

When I think about where this goes from an infrastructure perspective, I actually see a lot of the existing stack moving on-chain.

2. 应收账款的链上抵押

The second part is, going back to that whole 1–2–3 billion TPV that we do: is there a world where we can start collateralizing that on-chain? We basically take the receivables, put it on chain, get 60–70% against that on USDC. It doesn't remove our existing capital sources, but it helps supplement that with on-chain USDC.

When I think about where the infrastructure is going, I see the entire company stack moving onto stablecoin in the next 2–3 years. I think it's actually going to make it a much more efficient company that we can operate.

3. 可编程货币与 Agent

The last one — which we've been dabbling with right now — is basically thinking of programmable money. You have, obviously, the agents that are going to be doing purchasing from MCP, CLI, etc. But how do you make that something that's very usable for these different companies that we service?

Today we do have two agents that we sell. We have a lot more, but those are the ones that resonate the most:

  • Reconciliation agent — it basically looks at the purchases you have and helps you match them one-to-one.
  • GL coding agent — if you think about enterprises, a big function is literally someone's job is to look at every transaction and put a code on it so your ERP can read it. We trained an agent to be able to do that with 99% accuracy or better.

One of the huge advantages we have is the companies that we sell to look like Jeeves. We are operating in 12 countries. We have 60+ bank accounts. We have a sophisticated finance team. We operate in LatAm. So if our finance team can use it and they find it valuable, I'm pretty confident I can sell that product.

As I look at the next 2, 3, 4, 5 years, I see this convergence of stablecoin, I see the convergence of agents. Money is going to become programmable, and hopefully we're at that nexus of all of this happening.

十、创始人个人议题

不断重塑超能力

All right, I want to wrap maybe with a couple more personal questions about you. I think there's going to be more global businesses built. Huge opportunities there, very challenging. You've talked pretty publicly about how you need to almost reinvent your superpower every six to eight months. What do you think your superpower was when you started, and how is that evolving?

I think a lot of founders try to do a lot of different things when they start. The way I see it: your job is to focus on one or two maximum metrics that matter, and then you need to be like a monkey with a stick — how do you move that metric, and everything else doesn't matter. Once those metrics are moving, then you figure out other things to do.

I'll give you an example. When we started, our number one metric was spend. It's not revenue. It's not necessarily "does it have margin." It's: can I get someone to use this card in Latin America? I don't know — I did not grow up in Latin America. Actually, when I first flew to Mexico City, it was kind of dawning on me. I'm like, "I'm going to launch this product in Mexico. That was the second time I was…"

Spanish is a little rusty.

But that was the focus. It was just: can we get people to use the cards? It started there. We were clearly getting acceleration on the spend, and then we figured out all the other things — revenue, margin, etc. A lot of times founders try to do everything in the beginning, and in the beginning you need to just focus on what matters to get you from point A to point B.

70% 全能法则

The second part — which is where I think most founders need to really focus on — is you have to be 70% good at almost every function. And you just have to rotate. That's your job.

Today your job is product. Today I spent a lot of time on product engineering — it's just so important with stablecoin. Two years ago, it was finance, because we were navigating '23 and interest rates had shot up 600%. Every day I was on these calls, and that was a job. Now we have an amazing CFO, amazing finance team. So now I'm like, I don't need to spend that much time.

As a CEO, as a founder, your job is to do whatever job is needed for the company to get to the next stage. A lot of times founders are like, "Oh, I can't do sales." It's like, nobody can do sales. You just learn what you need to do. And then you hire someone better than you — that's also a skill. But you have to be dangerous enough to know what you're looking at. I think that's now even more easier with AI. If people can't do that now, I don't know what to say.

Yep. Even if you don't have an AI thesis, you should be able to lead.

It's amazing.

人才哲学

And then maybe — I think the other biggest challenge building a company anywhere is talent. How do you think about talent? What is Jeeves's philosophy, especially being in the number of countries that you're in?

We need local talent to succeed. That's the first hire we do in any geo. We actually in Argentina just hired this person — they are what we call a local GM. They have to be local. They have to know the market. They have to live there, because I'm not the one selling on the ground. So that's always the first hire.

What we look for — we have this internal bar, we call it relentlessness. There's no real playbook for what we're doing. There's different types of playbooks, but no one's launching this in 25 countries with multiple product stacks, and everyone's like, "Are you guys doing too much, and are you guys doing too little?" But that's what we do. That's what we offer. So you need to have this very high motor that is constantly just going.

The good thing with a company like Jeeves is you'll know within probably a week if this is the right fit. Because it's either going to be like, "Holy s***, I'm learning so much," or it's going to be like, "I don't know what's happening, I feel like I'm drowning every day." A lot of people cycle out fairly quickly, which I think is fine. It might not be the right fit. So we really push that engine part of like — you have to just have this high bar and keep going.

第二代领导团队

The second part here is: one thing we've been able to do is bring in good seasoned leaders for the second cycle of Jeeves. The first cycle is a zero-to-one cycle. This year we should be crossing hopefully 100 million in top-line ARR, and we need a second set of leaders. I think we've done a very good job, at least so far, of getting those seasoned leaders:

  • We brought in a CRO from Brex.
  • We brought in a risk officer from Capital One that joined us last year.
  • Our CFO took Pioneer public.

Getting those people to join has really upped the talent bar in the company as well.

十一、AI 原生 vs AI 改造：CEO 必须 AI-pilled

Very focused on stablecoins. You've also been chief AI officer of Jeeves. I think it is a huge advantage if you started pre-AI, you have the distribution, and then you can use that to really drive it forward. I think the challenge though is getting the company to be AI native. So maybe talk through: how do you make that happen?

It's a good question. Today we are at 140 people. We were at 200 people 2 years ago, and our revenue has grown 10x. Our volume has grown 8x. So clearly it's accelerating, and it's accelerating with less people. This just wouldn't be possible without AI.

So I'll walk through some concrete examples where we use it.

承销 / 客服 / KYB

Underwriting: We do pretty serious underwriting for any company that comes to us — because just like a Ramp or Brex, we have to give them 30 days of spend, and at the end of 30 days, they have to pay us back. So we need to know they're good to pay us back on day 31. Today we have a team of four people that does this underwriting across all these regions. That just simply wouldn't have been possible. Even two years ago, you needed 15 people just to get the underwriting going. Better models, self-learning models, self-training models. We have a lot of data, and now the models are actually keeping up with the data. That's a huge unlock.

Customer service: Same thing. We have maybe three people on the customer service team, and we have to handle different languages. We have to handle people that talked about payments, but then also cards, and also AP. We just are starting to test an AR product. It's a very small team. Wouldn't be possible without training what we have on the customer service side.

KYB and document ingestion: This is an area where we had to build our own version of an OCR machine. Even if you can do Spanish and Portuguese, there are little nuances. Like in Brazil, the commas and the dots are intertwined. If you look at something and it says $2,000, it's actually $200,000 because the comma and the dot is switched. It took us a while to train our models to be able to do that.

必须自有核心基础设施

Just like your question on the infrastructure, we want to own this core infrastructure ourselves. I think without that, we will not be able to succeed.

这件事必须 CEO 自己来

That to me falls on the founder and the CEO. If you are not AI-pilled, you're not going to make it.

Part of what I've been pushing internally is just: look, we are pre-AI, and we are trying to make that jump. If you think you're fast at 3x, there are native AI companies that are moving at 10x. So you're still slow. You need to be 20x just to keep up with the 10x.

Making that pace — if you don't have that pace yourself and you don't set that clock beat, it's not going to happen. You can't outsource this. You can't hire a chief AI person. You can hire that, and there are many very smart people. But that energy, that pace has to come from the CEO.

Thankfully, I have a little bit of a technical background. So I'm one of those maybe annoyingly dangerous CEOs where I'm like, "Oh, I'm good enough to tell you what to do, but then you can go figure out what to do." I feel so unblocked as an ex-engineer now with AI, because I feel like I can actually start building.

As soon as Open Claude came out, I built the first Open Claude for Jeeves — not because I'm some genius, but if I don't do it, everyone's going to say, "Hey, what's the security, etc." There are real security things you need to figure out. But if you don't show that you can build an Open Claude, and someone can tag it in a WhatsApp channel and it's going to give you jokes or whatever you want to do, then people just don't feel like they can do it themselves.

It's so important. Everybody says the same thing: you don't have enough time, etc. You have to make the time. If it's important for the company to succeed, you need AI, and if it's important to do AI, you need to figure out the time.

Candidly, it helps that it's actually very exciting building. It doesn't feel like a chore. I wish I had more time, because there's just so much I can do. Every week, it feels like Anthropic is releasing something else, and I'm like, "Give me one day so I can catch up to this."

收尾

Amazing. Well, thank you very much for joining us. I'm sure the great enterprises of Peru will be very happy to hear you can now economically launch there. Appreciate you coming on.

Thanks for having me. This is fantastic. And yeah, if there are companies out there that are looking to build with stablecoin and AI, we'd love for them to try us and give us feedback. Send an email directly to me, and I'll respond probably pretty quickly.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：「困难即护城河」其实是一种**反向时间套利**
Dileep 说"difficulty is defensible"，听起来像创业者的自我安慰，但仔细看 Jeeves 的数据会发现一个隐藏的非对称：**他们用 3 年时间建的「难做的基础设施」（自有 ledger、自有 BIN、25 国本地牌照），在 Stablecoin 时代变成了 3 个月就能撬动新市场的杠杆**。
这是真正的反直觉之处——Jeeves 看似在 2021–2023 那段时间"走慢路"，实则在偷偷积累一种「可重用的底层抽象」。当 Genius Act 通过、稳定币合法化的那一刻，他们的边际扩张成本从 70 万美元/8 个月骤降到「3 个销售人头」就能开一个秘鲁。
> **这其实是 AWS 的故事在金融业的重演**：亚马逊先为自己建了笨重的基础设施，然后把它变成了对外的商品。Jeeves 的 CMS（中间抽象层）就是它的 AWS——只是没人这么看它。
更出人意料的是：他们之所以能 4 个人做下 30 亿 TPV 的承销，**根源不在 AI 模型有多强，而在于他们 3 年前坚持自建 led
